name.md.title: name
name.md.body:
# name

**Part of Speech:** noun
**Level:** A1

**Definition:** 이름 — A word by which a person is known.

**Language of Origin:** Old English nama, Proto-Germanic *namô

## Examples

- "Hi, my name is ___" — 처음 만날 때
- "What's your name?" — 상대 이름 묻기
- "I never forget a name." — 좋은 인상의 신호

## Related Terms

- [[meet]] — to be introduced
- [[friend]] — someone you know well
- [[single]] — relationship status
- [[nice-to-meet-you]] — standard greeting

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — First introductions, ice-breakers
- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Elementary)
- Oxford 3000 (A1)

meet.md.title: meet
meet.md.body:
# meet

**Part of Speech:** verb
**Level:** A1

**Definition:** 만나다 — To be introduced to someone for the first time.

**Language of Origin:** Old English mētan, from Proto-Germanic *mōtijaną

## Examples

- "Nice to meet you." — standard first greeting
- "Have we met before?" — at a party/event
- "I'd love to meet your friends." — introducing your circle

## Related Terms

- [[name]] — identity
- [[friend]] — after meeting, you may become friends
- [[nice-to-meet-you]] — standard phrase

## Cultural Notes

In English, "nice to meet you" is said **once** at first meeting. After that
you use "Hi" / "Hey" / "Good to see you" — not "nice to meet you" again.

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — First introductions
- BBC Learning English — Greetings

single.md.title: single
single.md.body:
# single

**Part of Speech:** adjective
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** 솔로/미혼 — Not married; not in a romantic relationship.

## Examples

- "Are you single?" — 직접적 질문 (상황에 따라)
- "I'm happily single." — 즐기는 솔로
- "Single and ready to mingle." — dating 준비

## Related Terms

- [[friend]] — friend zone
- [[boyfriend]] / [[girlfriend]] — in a relationship
- [[relationship]] — committed

## Cultural Notes

**Direct question**: "Are you single?" can feel forward. Better:
- "Are you seeing anyone?" (less direct, more common)
- "Is there a significant other in your life?"

**LGBTQ+ context**: "single" is gender-neutral; "partner" preferred
for non-heteronormative relationships.

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — Relationship status questions
- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Pre-Intermediate)

beautiful.md.title: beautiful
beautiful.md.body:
# beautiful

**Part of Speech:** adjective
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** Pleasing the senses or mind aesthetically. Used for people, places, things, ideas, actions.

**Etymology:** From Old French "beau" (handsome) + "-ful" suffix. "Beau" itself comes from Latin "bellus" (pretty, charming).

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /ˈbjuːtɪfəl/
- Syllables: beau-ti-ful
- Stress: 1st syllable (BEAU-ti-ful)

**Memory Tip:** "**BEAUtiFUL**" — "Beau" = French word for handsome. French men are stereotypically handsome, so the word carries elegance. When you see a BEAU, the reaction is "ti-ful" (too beautiful)!

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "You're a beautiful" → missing noun ("You're a beautiful person")
- ❌ "Beautifuller" / "More beautiful" — both OK, but "more beautiful" is more natural
- ❌ "Beautiful girl" to a stranger → can feel like catcalling in many Western contexts
- ✅ "That's a beautiful painting" (art/object — safe)
- ✅ "You look beautiful tonight" (event context — appropriate)
- Note: For men, use [[handsome]]. "Beautiful boy" sounds effeminate or ironic.

**Register:** semi-formal. Universally appropriate but considered slightly elevated/cordial.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 English words)

**Visual:** ```
  B·EAU  →  T I  →  F·UL
  (French "beau")  (suffix)
```

## Examples

- "You look beautiful tonight." — 칭찬, formal context (date, event)
- "What a beautiful day." — 일상, weather/scenes
- "Beautiful work on the project!" — 성과 칭찬, business
- "That's a beautiful name." — 개인적 호감 표현
- "Beautiful mind, beautiful soul." — 추상적, 시적

## Mini-Dialogue

```
A: Wow, this sunset is beautiful!   ← 와, 이 노을 정말 아름답다!
B: It really is. The colors are amazing.   ← 정말이야. 색깔이 놀랍다.
A: Want to take a photo together?   ← 같이 사진 찍을래?
B: I'd love that.   ← 좋지.
```

## Related Terms

- [[handsome]] — used for men
- [[cute]] — younger, playful vibe, less formal
- [[pretty]] — slightly less formal than beautiful; can describe people/art
- [[gorgeous]] — stronger compliment; "stunning"
- [[lovely]] — softer, more British; less intense
- [[attractive]] — neutral, can be physical/abstract

## Cultural Notes

- **Eye contact + smile** while saying "you look beautiful" is essential — alone it can feel creepy.
- In dating apps: "beautiful" is overused. "Gorgeous" or "stunning" stands out more.
- For compliments on appearance, **specify what** (dress, hair, eyes) — more sincere than generic.
- **Avoid** to strangers — in many cultures it's interpreted as harassment.

## Sources

- [[english-dating-culture]] — Compliments (appearance)
- Oxford Word Skills (Intermediate)
- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Upper-Int)

handsome.md.title: handsome
handsome.md.body:
# handsome

**Part of Speech:** adjective
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** (Of a man) good-looking, attractive. Can also apply to objects (a handsome building) or amounts (a handsome sum).

**Etymology:** From "hand" + Old English "sum" (easy, ready to hand). Originally meant "easy to handle, near at hand." Sense of "good-looking" developed ~16th century.

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /ˈhænsəm/
- Syllables: hand-some
- Stress: 1st syllable (HAND-some)
- Note: "ds" is silent in pronunciation

**Memory Tip:** A **HAND-SOME** man is good enough to **handle** (a room, a date, a job interview). Confident, capable, attractive.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "She's handsome" → traditional rule: women = [[beautiful]]/[[pretty]], men = handsome
- ❌ "Handsomely" (as adjective) → "handsomely" is the adverb ("He was handsomely dressed")
- ❌ "A handsome" alone → needs a noun ("a handsome man" / "a handsome face")
- ✅ "He's really handsome." (describing a man)
- ✅ "What a handsome couple." (couple = mixed, both look good)
- ✅ "A handsome profit." (large, generous — different meaning!)
- Note: Modern usage is **more flexible** — "handsome woman" is becoming acceptable, especially for non-binary or strong-featured. But "beautiful" is safer default.

**Register:** semi-formal. Standard compliment.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐

## Examples

- "He's really handsome." — 외모 칭찬
- "What a handsome couple." — 부부/커플 칭찬
- "Handsome and charming." — 종합 칭찬
- "A handsome salary." — 넉넉한 급여 (different meaning)
- "A handsome building." — 웅장한 건물 (object)

## Mini-Dialogue (Complimenting)

```
A: That's a nice photo of your boyfriend.   ← 남자친구 사진 잘 나왔네.
B: Thanks! He's pretty handsome, right?   ← 고마워! 꽤 잘생기지, 맞아?
A: Definitely. And he looks kind too.   ← 확실히. 그리고 친절해 보여.
B: He really is. The total package.   ← 진짜 그래. 완벽해.
```

## Related Terms

- [[beautiful]] — usually for women / general beauty
- [[cute]] — playful, younger vibe; can apply to men
- [[pretty]] — women, less intense than beautiful
- [[gorgeous]] — stronger than handsome; "wow factor"
- [[attractive]] — neutral, can be physical/personality
- [[good-looking]] — casual alternative to handsome
- [[hunky]] — slang, very handsome (often muscular)
- [[cute]] / [[hot]] — younger generation, casual

## Cultural Notes

- Traditionally: **handsome = men**, **beautiful = women**, **pretty = women or gender-neutral**. Modern usage is more flexible.
- **Avoid overusing** "handsome" — feels generic. Specific compliments feel more genuine:
  - "You have a great smile."
  - "I love your sense of style."
  - "Your eyes are striking."
- **"Handsome" can describe objects** — a handsome car, a handsome profit. Old-fashioned but elegant.
- **Dating apps**: "handsome" is overused. Be specific to stand out.
- **Age factor**: "handsome" sounds slightly old-fashioned; younger people use "cute" or "hot" more.

## Sources

- [[english-dating-culture]] — Compliments (appearance)
- [[beautiful]] — comparison (women)
- Oxford Word Skills (Intermediate)

kind.md.title: kind
kind.md.body:
# kind

**Part of Speech:** adjective
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** Having or showing a friendly, generous, considerate nature. Describes consistent behavior toward others.

**Etymology:** Old English "gecynde" (natural, native). Sense of "good-natured" developed ~13th century. Cognate with "kind" (noun, meaning type/category) — both from "kin" (family).

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /kaɪnd/
- Syllables: kind (1 syllable)
- Note: Same pronunciation as "kind" (type) — context disambiguates
- "Kind to me" = adjective (good-natured)
- "What kind?" = noun (type)

**Memory Tip:** "**KIND**" person is a **KIN**dred soul (family-like). You treat them like family = kind. Or: "Kin-d" → family + goodness = kindness.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "You're kind" alone → can sound like a diagnosis ("You have a kind heart" — awkward)
- ❌ "Kind person" (always with article) → "You're a kind person"
- ❌ "She is very kindly" (as adjective) → "She is very kind" (kind is the adjective; kindly is the adverb)
- ✅ "That's so kind of you." (gratitude — most common use)
- ✅ "He's kind to everyone." (describing behavior)
- ✅ "Kind words" / "kind thoughts" (noun use — types of)
- Note: "Kind" describes **action**, not appearance. For appearance use [[handsome]], [[beautiful]].

**Register:** universally appropriate, sincere. Slightly elevated in writing.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 English words)

## Examples

- "You're really kind." — 성격 칭찬
- "That's so kind of you." — 감사 (very common response to help)
- "He's kind to everyone." — 일관된 친절
- "It was kind of strange." — 약간 (= "sort of" — different meaning!)
- "Kind regards" — 이메일/편지 맺음말

## Mini-Dialogue (Friend)

```
A: Thanks for helping me move today!   ← 오늘搬家 도와줘서 고마워!
B: No problem. That's what friends are for.   ← 천만에. 그게 친구하는 일이지.
A: You're really kind, you know that?   ← 너 진짜 친절하다, 알지?
B: Aw, you're making me blush.   ← 에이, 부끄럽잖아.
```

## Related Terms

- [[nice]] — weaker, more general; "nice" is overused
- [[good]] — broader (kind of person, good at math, good food)
- [[sweet]] — warmer, more affectionate; "That's sweet of you"
- [[generous]] — emphasizes giving, sharing
- [[considerate]] — thinks of others' needs
- [[gentle]] — soft-spoken, mild
- [[caring]] — actively takes care of others

## Cultural Notes

- "Kind" is consistently rated one of the **most attractive traits** in English-speaking dating surveys (above "smart" or "funny" for long-term relationships).
- A **sincere compliment** — describes consistent behavior, not just appearance.
- **"Kind of"** (adjective + of) = "sort of" (somewhat). Different meaning. "I'm kind of tired" = "I'm somewhat tired."
- **"Kind regards"** is a professional sign-off — common in business emails.
- "Be kind" has become a cultural movement (mental health awareness, social media).
- **Polite culture**: "That's so kind" is a common response to ANY small favor. Reinforces social bonds.

## Sources

- [[english-dating-culture]] — Compliments (personality)
- [[nice]] — comparison
- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Pre-Intermediate)

nice-to-meet-you.md.body:
# nice-to-meet-you

**Part of Speech:** expression (greeting)
**Definition:** "만나서 반가워요" — Standard polite greeting on first meeting.

**Form:** Nice to meet you.

## Usage

처음 만났을 때 한 번. 그 후로는 "Hi" / "Hey" / "Good to see you".

## Variations

- **Nice to meet you.** — 정중, 격식
- **Nice meeting you.** — 헤어질 때 (변형)
- **Pleased to meet you.** — 격식, 비즈니스
- **Good to meet you.** — 캐주얼

## Formal vs Casual

- Formal: "It's a pleasure to meet you."
- Casual: "Hey, nice to meet ya!"

## Cultural Notes

**Only said once**. Repeating "nice to meet you" on a second
meeting is awkward. Use "Hi again" or "Good to see you" instead.

## Sources

- Cambridge English Vocabulary in Use (Elementary)

want-coffee.md.body:
# want-coffee

**Part of Speech:** expression (invitation)
**Definition:** "커피 한 잔 할래요?" — Common low-pressure date invitation.

**Form:** Want to grab a coffee? / Would you like to get coffee?

## Usage

가장 흔한 첫 데이트 제안. 부담 적고, 공공장소, 대화하기 좋은 분위기.

- **Can I buy you a coffee?** — 비용 부담 명시 (호의)

## Cultural Notes

**Coffee date = default first date** in US/UK. Implies interest
without heavy commitment. Public place, easy exit, low cost.

## Related Expressions

- **Let's hang out** — ambiguous (friends OR date)
- **Dinner sometime?** — 더 격식

be-my-girlfriend.md.title: be-my-girlfriend
be-my-girlfriend.md.body:
# be-my-girlfriend

**Part of Speech:** expression (confession, formal)
**Definition:** "내 여자친구 돼줘" — Formal proposal for exclusive relationship.

**Form:** Will you be my girlfriend? / Will you go out with me?

## Usage

**공식 (gōshiki) 고백 단계**. 호감 표현을 넘어 교제 시작을 명시.

## Variations

- **Will you be my girlfriend?** — 표준
- **Will you go out with me?** — 더 캐주얼
- **Would you be my girlfriend?** — 좀 더 정중
- **Wanna be my girlfriend?** — 매우 캐주얼

## Cultural Notes

This is a **commitment proposal**, not a casual question. Asking
this implies you've already been dating and want to formalize.

**Gender note**: "Be my girlfriend" = male asking female. For
same-sex, use "partner". For reverse direction: "Will you be my
boyfriend?"

## Related Expressions

- [[really-like-you]] — 감정 표현
- [[i-love-you]] — 확정 후
- [[hold-your-hand]] — physical progression

hold-your-hand.md.title: hold-your-hand
hold-your-hand.md.body:
# hold-your-hand

**Part of Speech:** expression (intimacy request)
**Definition:** "손 잡아도 돼?" — Consent request for first physical affection.

**Form:** Can I hold your hand?

## Usage

**신체 친밀의 첫 단계** + **명시적 동의** 요청. 현대 영어권의 매너.

## Variations

- **Can I hold your hand?** — 정중
- **Want to hold my hand?** — 캐주얼
- **May I hold your hand?** — 격식

## Cultural Notes

**Consent is the standard**: Modern dating ethics in English-speaking
countries require **explicit verbal permission** before physical
intimacy. "Can I hold your hand?" is **expected, not awkward**.

**Not asking** = potential red flag.

**Earlier stage**: "Want to walk together?" (just being close).
Then: holding hands.
Then: arm around waist.
Then: kiss (also with consent).

## Related Expressions

- [[kiss]] — next step
- [[really-like-you]] — emotional context
- [[be-my-girlfriend]] — relationship status

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — Affection progression
- Modern dating ethics literature

english-dating-culture.md.title: English Dating Culture
english-dating-culture.md.body:
# English Dating Culture

**Type:** romance, social-ritual
**Region:** US / UK / Canada / Australia (English-speaking countries)

## Overview

English-speaking countries (especially US) have **explicit, individualist** dating culture. Romantic intent is usually stated clearly, consent is verbal, and personal boundaries are respected. Modern dating emphasizes direct communication, gender equality (in principle), and emotional honesty.

## Setting

A first date in the US/UK: **coffee shop, restaurant, bar, or walk in a park**. Evening (7-9pm typical). Casual to smart-casual dress. Two people meeting with romantic intent, getting to know each other.

## Roles

- **Person who invites (inviter)** — takes initiative, suggests time/place
- **Invitee** — can accept, suggest alternatives, or decline politely
- Both pay attention to comfort, consent, and clear communication

## Scenario: A Typical First Date

1. **Planning** — Inviter suggests a specific time/place ("Want to grab coffee Saturday at 3?")
2. **Acceptance** — Invitee confirms or reschedules
3. **Arrival** — Both arrive on time (5 min early is normal); greet with smile + light hug or wave
4. **Conversation** — Mix of light (hobbies, work) and deeper (values, life goals) topics
5. **Physical contact** — Wait for clear signals; ask before initiating hand-holding or kissing
6. **Bill** — Often offered by inviter, but splitting is normal; "Let's split?" is OK
7. **End** — Confirm interest: "I had a great time, want to do this again?"
8. **Follow-up text** — Within 24 hours: "Thanks for tonight, I really enjoyed it."

## Cultural Insight

### "Hanging Out" vs "Dating"

In US English, "date" implies **explicit romantic intent**. "Hang out" is ambiguous — could be friends or romantic. Modern dating advice encourages **clarifying intent early** to avoid misunderstandings.

**"Are we hanging out or dating?"** is a real conversation that happens. Best to be clear from the start.

### Splitting the Bill

**Generation gap**:
- Older generations: man traditionally pays
- Younger (Z, Millennials): 50/50 split is normal
- Most common: person who invited pays, but offering to split is polite

**Tactful**: Don't insist either way. "Let's split it?" or "I've got this one" are both fine.

### Consent Culture

Modern English-speaking countries (especially US/Canada/UK) have embraced **explicit verbal consent**:
- "Can I hold your hand?"
- "Can I kiss you?"
- "Is this okay?"

Not asking is now considered a **red flag**. Asking is **mature**, not awkward.

### "I Love You" Timing

- **US**: typically 2-3 months into a relationship
- **UK**: often later (3-6 months)
- **Australia**: relatively early (1-2 months)

Earlier expression: "I really like you" (more universally safe).

### Personal Space

- **Friends/acquaintances**: ~45cm (18in)
- **Romantic partners**: closer, touching common
- **Strangers**: arm's length+

**Eye contact**: 60-70% during conversation = interested.

### Online / Text Dating

- **Apps**: Tinder, Bumble, Hinge dominant in US/UK
- **First contact**: text/messaging common
- **Phone call**: more sincere, less common
- **Asking out**: text is fine; in-person or call for serious intent

## Useful Phrases

- [[i-like-you]] — early affection
- [[i-love-you]] — deep feeling
- [[beautiful]] — appearance compliment
- [[handsome]] — male appearance compliment
- [[kind]] — personality compliment
- [[can-i-kiss-you]] — consent request
- [[would-you-like-to]] — invitation
- [[i-had-a-great-time]] — end-of-date
- [[see-you-again]] — follow-up

## What NOT to Do

- ❌ **"Wanna date?"** in first message — too direct
- ❌ **Bombarding texts** — turn-off
- ❌ **"You up?"** late-night texts — usually means casual sex
- ❌ **Love bombing** — excessive flattery early
- ❌ **Ghosting** — disappearing without explanation (avoidable but common)
- ❌ **"You're so beautiful"** to a stranger on the street — catcalling, harassment
- ❌ **Asking weight, age, salary** on first date — too personal
- ❌ **Talking only about yourself** — reciprocal conversation expected

## Body Language

| Gesture | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Eye contact 60-70% | Interested, engaged |
| Mirroring (leaning in, gestures) | Comfortable, in sync |
| Looking at phone during date | Disinterested, rude |
| Fidgeting, looking around | Nervous or bored |
| Touching face/hair (other person) | Often flirting, but not always |
| Hand on table palm up | Inviting touch |
| Turning body toward you | Engaged |

## Cross-Reference

- Compare with: [[japanese-dating-culture]], [[spanish-dating-culture]], [[korean-dating-culture]]
- See also: [[i-love-you]], [[beautiful]], [[handsome]]

## Holiday / Special Days

- **Valentine's Day** (Feb 14) — big romantic holiday
- **Anniversaries** — celebrate relationship start
- **No formal "100 days" celebration** (unlike Korean culture)

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — main source
- BBC Learning English — Relationships
- The Atlantic — Modern dating essays
- OKCupid / Hinge dating blog posts
- Consent culture resources (Planned Parenthood, etc.)

i-love-you.md.title: i-love-you
i-love-you.md.body:
# i-love-you

**Part of Speech:** expression (deep confession)
**Definition:** "사랑해" — Deep romantic confession. Most weighted phrase in English romance.

**Form:** I love you.

## Usage

**English romance의 정점**. 이전 단계: "I like you" → "I really
like you" → "I love you". 점진적 상승.

## Variations

- **I love you.** — 표준, 정중
- **I love ya.** — 캐주얼 (친구, 가족, 연인 모두)
- **I'm in love with you.** — 더 깊음 (감정적 깊이 강조)
- **Love you!** — 일상적 작별 인사 (영어권)

## Cultural Notes

**Timing matters**:
- US: typically 2-3 months in
- UK: often later
- Australia: relatively early

**Saying it too early** can be uncomfortable. **Receiving it
unexpectedly** can be a relationship-defining moment.

**Same-sex usage**: identical.

## Related Expressions

- [[really-like-you]] — lighter version
- [[be-my-girlfriend]] — formal commitment
- [[stay-friends]] — if not reciprocated

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — Affection progression
- Notting Hill (1999) — iconic scene

really-like-you.md.title: really-like-you
really-like-you.md.body:
# really-like-you

**Part of Speech:** expression (confession, partial)
**Definition:** "난 정말 너를 좋아해" — Strong but not full confession.

**Form:** I really like you.

## Usage

호감 표현의 중간 단계. "like" 보다 강하지만 "love" 전 단계.

## Variations

- **I like you.** — 약한 호감
- **I really like you.** — 확고한 호감
- **I'm falling for you.** — 빠지는 중
- **I like you a lot.** — 비슷한 강도

## Cultural Notes

This is a **safe confession** for early stages. Less weight than
"I love you" but clearer than just "I like you". Many people use
this as the actual "I love you" precursor in serious relationships.

## Related Expressions

- [[i-love-you]] — the next step
- [[be-my-girlfriend]] — formal proposal
- [[hold-your-hand]] — physical progression

## Sources

- [[dating-romance]] — Confession progression
- The Notebook (2004) — "I am who I am because of you"

**Part of Speech:** 名詞 (noun)
**Level:** N5

## Examples

- 「お名前は何ですか?」 — What is your name?

- 「君」(くん) — 친근 (윗사람 → 아랫사람)
- 「ちゃん」 — 친근, 애칭 (어린이, 여자친구)

綺麗.md.title: 綺麗
綺麗.md.body:
# 綺麗

**Part of Speech:** 形容詞 (な-adjective)
**Level:** N4

**Definition:** Beautiful (people, things, scenery); clean; pure. Two main senses: (1) visual beauty, (2) cleanliness/order.

**読み方:** きれい (kirei)

**Etymology:** 綺 (beautiful brocade) + 麗 (elegant). Originally described ornate textiles; shifted to general beauty by Heian period (8-12c).

**Pronunciation:**
- 読み方: きれい (kirei)
- Pitch: 頭高 (L-H-H-H) — き↓れ↑い↑  / alternatively H-H-H-H
- For 奇麗: きれい (same reading, kanji variant)
- For 美: うつくしい (utsukushii) — formal/literary

**Memory Tip:** 綺麗 → KIREI → "**KI**nd **RE**flection **I**magined" — 3 syllables, the i-adj echo of "kire-i". Or: "**K**eep **I**t **R**eally **E**legant **I**n mind" = きれい (beautiful).

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ 「綺麗の人」 → 「綺麗な人」(na-adj needs な before noun)
- ❌ 「綺麗じゃないで」 → 「綺麗じゃないです」(polite form)
- ❌ 「綺麗」to a stranger → feels too forward; reserve for established relationship
- ❌ 「綺麗でした」 → 「綺麗でした」OK for past events, but 「綺麗ですね」 more natural
- ✅ 「綺麗ですね」(present compliment — natural)
- ✅ 「部屋が綺麗」(clean room — different sense)
- ✅ 「綺麗に書きます」(do something beautifully/cleanly — adverbial にも)
- Note: For men use かっこいい or ハンサム (handsome), not 綺麗.

**Register:** standard polite. Universally appropriate within established relationships.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 JP words)

**Visual:**
```
   綺 ← silk, brocade (8 strokes)
       ↓
   麗 ← elegant, beautiful (19 strokes, complex)
   ─────────
   綺麗 = ornate + elegant = beautiful
```

## Examples

- 「綺麗ですね」 — You're beautiful (compliment, neutral politeness)
- 「部屋が綺麗」 — The room is clean
- 「綺麗な花」 — Beautiful flower
- 「綺麗にしてください」 — Please make it clean / please clean it up
- 「お皿を綺麗にする」 — To clean the dishes (literally: make them beautiful)
- 「綺麗に書く」 — To write neatly/cleanly

## Mini-Dialogue (Compliment)

```
A: 今日、可愛いですね。   ← 오늘, 예쁘시네요.
B: ありがとう！ 綺麗って言ってくれて嬉しい。   ← 고마워! 綺麗라고 해줘서 기뻐.
A: 本当のことだよ。   ← 진심이야.
B: (笑) あなたもハンサムだよ。   ← (웃음) 당신도 잘생겼어요.
A: ありがとう。嬉しい。   ← 고마워. 기뻐.
```

## Variations

- 綺麗(きれい) — na-adj form, common
- 奇麗(きれい) — same reading, variant kanji (less common)
- 美(うつく)しい — formal/literary, "utsukushii"
- ビューティフル — katakana loanword, casual

## Related Terms

- [[可愛い]] — cute (younger, playful)
- [[優しい]] — kind
- [[かっこいい]] — cool/handsome (for men)
- [[ハンサム]] — handsome (loanword)
- [[美しい]] — beautiful (formal/literary)
- 美しい — kanji-only writing
- 清楚(せいそ) — pure, innocent (literary)

## Cultural Notes

- **주의**: 綺麗를 처음 만난 사람에게 즉시 사용하면 가벼워 보일 수 있음. **설정된 관계에서** 진심 어린 칭찬으로 사용.
- 여성에게 가장 흔한 외모 칭찬. 남성에게는 かっこいい 또는 ハンサム.
- **두 가지 의미** — beauty와 cleanliness. Context가 구분: 「部屋が綺麗」= clean room, 「花が綺麗」= beautiful flower.
- 「綺麗に〜する」패턴이 매우 생산적: 「綺麗にかける」(to write neatly), 「綺麗に食べる」(to eat everything cleanly), 「綺麗に忘れる」(to cleanly forget — emotional distance).
- **일본 특유의 미의식**: 綺麗 includes moral purity (綺麗好き = clean-freak, 綺麗な心 = pure heart).

## Sources

- [[japanese-dating-culture]] — 칭찬
- TRY! N4 語彙
- みんなの日本語 中級

可愛い.md.title: 可愛い
可愛い.md.body:
# 可愛い

**Part of Speech:** 形容詞 (い-adjective)
**Level:** N4

**Definition:** Cute; adorable; charming. Used for people, animals, things, behaviors. A central word in Japanese pop culture.

**読み方:** かわいい (kawaii)

**Etymology:** Originally 可愛(かは)し (kahashi) = "embarrassed, pitiable" (Heian period). 顔(かほ)し (kahoshi) = "lovely face." Shifted to "cute" in 20th century; popularized by Sanrio (Hello Kitty, 1970s).

**Pronunciation:**
- 読み方: かわいい (kawaii)
- Pitch: 頭高 (H-L-L-L-L) — か↓わ↑い↑い↑  / also L-H-H-H acceptable
- Type: い-adjective (可愛い,  可愛くない,  可愛かった)

**Memory Tip:** "**Kawaii**" = 5 letters, kawaii. English speakers learn this word first! 「**KA**me **WAI** (turtle) **I**」→ A cute turtle. Or: the sound mimics the cuteness — high-pitched, soft ending.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ 「可愛い人」 alone → actually OK ("a cute person"), but ambiguous
- ❌ 「可愛くありません」 → 「可愛くありません」or 「可愛くないです」(i-adj, no な)
- ❌ 「可愛いね」to a man → sounds like calling him girly/childish, possibly offensive
- ❌ 「可愛い」 to mean "sexy/hot" → WRONG; use 魅力的 or セクシー
- ✅ 「可愛いね」(casual compliment — universal)
- ✅ 「可愛い赤ちゃん」(cute baby — standard)
- ✅ 「可愛い性格だね」(cute personality — describes endearing traits)
- Note: For men, use ハンサム or かっこいい, NOT 可愛い. Exception: when describing specific cute behavior (a childlike smile) is fine.

**Register:** casual to semi-formal. Universally appropriate in casual contexts.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (cultural icon, top 300 JP words)

**Visual:**
```
   可 = can/may
       ↓
   愛 = love, affection
   ─────────
   可愛い = "lovable-able" = cute
```

## Examples

- 「可愛いね」 — You're cute (casual compliment)
- 「可愛い服」 — Cute clothes
- 「可愛い赤ちゃん」 — Cute baby
- 「可愛いキャラクター」 — Cute character
- 「可愛く撮って」(Take a cute photo of me) — modern phrase
- 「あの子の笑顔、可愛いな」(That kid's smile is cute)

## Mini-Dialogue (Casual)

```
A: この猫、見て！   ← 이 고양이, 봐!
B: えっ、めっちゃ可愛い！   ← 뭐야, 진짜 귀엽다!
A: ね、触ってもいい？   ← 그렇지, 만져봐도 돼?
B: うん、優しくね。   ← 응, 살살.
A: (猫がなでなで)   ← (고양이 쓰다듬기)
B: 幸せそう...   ← 행복해 보인다...
```

## Variations

- 可愛(かわい)い — standard
- 愛(いと)らしい — elegant/endearing, more literary
- チャーミング — katakana (charming)
- カワイイ — katakana (kawaii, pop culture)

## Related Terms

- [[綺麗]] — beautiful (more elegant/adult)
- [[優しい]] — kind
- [[面白い]] — funny/interesting
- [[ハンサム]] — handsome
- [[かっこいい]] — cool (often men)
- [[美人]] — beautiful woman
- [[アイドル]] — idol (kawaii aesthetic)

## Cultural Notes

- **일본 문화에서 "kawaii"는 매우 광범위한 칭찬** — 외모, 행동, 물건, 캐릭터 모두에 사용. 글로벌 문화에서도 통용.
- **외모 칭찬으로 사용 시**: 여성/아이에게 자연스러움. 남성에게는 상황에 따라 (귀여운 행동 묘사 시).
- **카와이 문화**: Hello Kitty, Sanrio, 일본 패션, 만화. 사회적 미의식의 큰 축.
- **카와이 시점**: 어떤 것이 cute한지 — 캐릭터, 디자인, 행동. 단순히 "예쁘다"보다 더 큰 범위.
- **남성에게**: 절대적으로 부적절하지는 않지만, "아이같다"는 뉘앙스가 있어 사용 자제. 부부의 사적인 대화에 한해 OK.
- **"Kawaii desu ne"** (可愛いですね) = almost a cultural greeting when seeing something cute.

## Sources

- [[japanese-dating-culture]] — 칭찬
- 日本語中級J501
- Sanrio history (1970s)
- 日本語ジャーナル (various issues)

**Part of Speech:** 形容詞 (adjective, い-adjective)
**Level:** N4

**Definition:** 멋있다/멋진 — Cool; good-looking (for men); stylish.

**読み方:** かっこいい (kakkoii)

## Examples

優しい.md.title: 優しい
優しい.md.body:
# 優しい

**Part of Speech:** 形容詞 (い-adjective)
**Level:** N4

**Definition:** Kind; gentle; considerate. Describes people, words, actions, things.

**読み方:** やさしい (yasashii)

**Etymology:** 優(やさ) = "gentle, superior" + しい = adjective ending. Old Japanese also 優(ゆ)し = same meaning. Originally described noble/refined behavior; shifted to general kindness.

**Pronunciation:**
- 読み方: やさしい (yasashii)
- Pitch: 頭高 (H-L-L-L) — や↓さ↑し↑い↑  / also L-H-H-H
- Type: い-adjective

**Memory Tip:** "**Y**ou're **A** **S**o **A** **SHI**" — "you are a 'sashi' (gentle spirit)". The 'sa-shi-i' rhythm feels soft, like kindness. Or: 優しい → "**Y**es, **A**-**S**mooth **I**-nteraction" = kind.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ 「優しい人」 alone → OK grammatically, but 「優しい人だね」more natural
- ❌ 「優しくありません」 → 「優しくないです」(i-adj polite negative)
- ❌ 「優しいね」to a stranger → too forward (sounds like flirting)
- ❌ Confusing with 易しい(やさしい) — "easy (to understand)" — SAME pronunciation!
  - 「優しい先生」= kind teacher
  - 「易しい問題」= easy question
- ✅ 「優しい人」(kind person)
- ✅ 「彼、彼女は優しい」(He/she is kind)
- ✅ 「優しくしてほしい」(I want to be treated gently)
- Note: 「優しい」= consistent kindness, not just one action. For single acts, use 優しいね more casually.

**Register:** standard polite. Universally appropriate when sincere.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 300 JP words)

**Visual:**
```
   優 = "person (人) + worry (尤)"
       "person worried about others" = kind
   ─────────
   優しい = kind, gentle (action-focused)
```

## Examples

- 「優しい人」 — Kind person
- 「彼はとても優しい」 — He is very kind
- 「優しくしてください」 — Please be gentle
- 「優しい言葉」 — Kind words
- 「優しい性格」 — Kind personality
- 「ご親切に」(thank you for your kindness — formal)

## Mini-Dialogue (Compliment)

```
A: 今日は寒いね。   ← 오늘 춥다.
B: どうぞ、私のコート。   ← 자, 제 코트요.
A: えっ、いいよ。   ← 뭐, 괜찮아.
B: いいから、着て。優しいんだよ、私。   ← 괜찮다니까, 입어. 착한 사람이야, 나는.
A: (笑) ありがとう。   ← (웃음) 고마워.
B: (優しく微笑む)   ← (다정하게 미소짓다)
```

## Variations

- 優(やさ)しい — standard, common
- 親切(しんせつ) — kind (different nuance: more active helpfulness)
- 温(あたた)かい — warm (different: emotional warmth)
- 穏(おだ)やか — calm, gentle (personality)
- 大人(おとな)しい — quiet, gentle (behavior)
- 可愛(かわい)い — cute (different: endearing)

## Related Terms

- [[面白い]] — funny
- [[綺麗]] — beautiful
- [[好き]] — like
- 親切 — kind (helpful)
- 温かい — warm
- 穏やか — calm
- 大人しい — quiet
- 厳しい — strict (antonym)

## Cultural Notes

- **외모보다 인성을 칭찬할 때 사용**. 일본에서 인성 칭찬은 진심으로 받아들여지는 안전한 표현.
- **상대가 누구에게**: "優しいね"는 여자친구/남자친구 사이에서 매우 흔한 칭찬.
- **일본어의 뉘앙스**:
  - 優しい = consistent, habitual kindness
  - 親切 = situational helpfulness (e.g., helpful staff)
  - 温かい = emotional warmth, hospitality
- **발음 함정**: 易しい(やさしい) "easy"와 동음. 문맥으로 구분.
- **데일리에서 "優しい" 칭찬의 무게**: 매우 진지한 호감 표현 — "I see how kind you are" = trust signal.
- **부모→자녀**: 일본 부모가 자녀에게 가장 많이 쓰는 칭찬 중 하나.

## Sources

- [[japanese-dating-culture]] — 칭찬
- 日本語中級J501
- TRY! N4 文法

面白い.md.title: 面白い
面白い.md.body:
# 面白い

**Part of Speech:** 形容詞 (い-adjective)
**Level:** N4

**Definition:** Interesting; funny; amusing; enjoyable. Combines English "interesting" + "funny" + "enjoyable" in one word.

**読み方:** おもしろい (omoshiroi)

**Etymology:** 面(おも) = "face, surface" + 白(しろ)い = "white." Originally meant "having a clear/bright face" → vivid, distinctive. Shifted to "interesting" by Edo period (17-19c).

**Pronunciation:**
- 読み方: おもしろい (omoshiroi)
- Pitch: 平板 (L-L-L-H-H) — おもしろ↓い↑ (flat-low → い high)
- Type: い-adjective

**Memory Tip:** "**O**ne **MOSHI** **RO**und **I**nteresting" — when something hits 面白い, you want one more round. Or: "面白(おもしろう)い" = "face becomes white" = surprised face = interesting!

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ 「面白いです」 → 「面白いですね」(more natural with sentence-end particle)
- ❌ 「面白くない人」 → sounds harsh; prefer 「話が合わない」(we don't click)
- ❌ 「面白くありません」→ 「面白くないです」(i-adj, polite form)
- ❌ 「面白い人」 to mean "weird person" → 「面白い人」= positive (funny/interesting); use 「変な人」for weird
- ✅ 「面白い映画だった」(It was an interesting movie)
- ✅ 「彼、面白いね」(He's funny, isn't he)
- ✅ 「話が面白い」(The conversation is interesting)
- Note: 「面白い」can be **negative** if context is ironic: 「本当に面白いね」with sarcastic tone = "wow, how 'interesting'" (sarcastic).

**Register:** standard polite. Universally appropriate.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 JP words)

**Visual:**
```
   面 = face, mask (9 strokes)
       ↓
   白 = white, plain (5 strokes)
   ─────────
   面白い = "face is white" = surprised, vivid, interesting
```

## Examples

- 「面白い人」 — Interesting/funny person
- 「面白い映画」 — Interesting movie
- 「話が面白い」 — Story/conversation is interesting
- 「面白いことない？」 — Nothing interesting happening? (asking)
- 「面白い話があるよ」 — I have an interesting story

## Mini-Dialogue (Date Idea)

```
A: 今週末、何する？   ← 이번 주말, 뭐 해?
B: 面白い映画があるんだ、見に行かない？   ← 재미있는 영화 있는데, 보러 갈래?
A: いいね！ 何の映画？   ← 좋아! 무슨 영화?
B: 韓国映画なんだけど... 試してみる？   ← 한국 영화인데... 시도해볼래?
A: もちろん！楽しみ。   ← 당연하지! 기대돼.
```

## Variations

- 面白(おもしろ)い — standard
- 楽(たの)しい — enjoyable (different nuance: more emotional/fun)
- 愉快(ゆかい) — cheerful, amusing (literary)
- 興味深い(きょうみぶかい) — intellectually interesting
- 笑(わら)える — laughable, funny (specific to humor)

## Related Terms

- [[優しい]] — kind
- [[綺麗]] — beautiful
- [[カフェ]] — café (for date ideas)
- 楽しい — enjoyable
- 退屈(たいくつ) — boring (antonym)
- つまらない — boring (colloquial)
- 笑える — funny
- 興味深い — fascinating

## Cultural Notes

- **일본어에서 「面白い」= 재미있다/즐겁다/흥미로운** 모두 포함. 영어 "funny" + "interesting" + "enjoyable" 통합.
- 사람을 묘사할 때: **분위기 메이커, 대화가 잘 통하는 사람** 신호. 데이트에서 매우 중요한 매력.
- **"He/she is 面白い"** = they make you laugh, you want to spend more time with them, they have stories to tell.
- **음식에 사용**: 「この料理、面白い味だね」= "interesting taste" (not bad, but unusual).
- **드라마, 만화에서**: 거의 모든 "funny" 상황이 面白い로 표현됨.
- **비판적 사용**: 「本当に面白くないね」 = sarcastic, very cutting.

## Sources

- [[japanese-dating-culture]] — 칭찬
- TRY! N4 文法
- 日本語中級J501

처음 만났을 때 사용. "처음 뵙겠습니다"와 같은 격식. 그 후 "よろしくお願いします"로 이어짐.

# 好きです付き合ってください

**Part of Speech:** 表現 (expression, formal confession)
**Definition:** "좋아합니다, 사귀어 주세요" — Standard formal confession.

**Form:** 好きです。付き合ってください。

## Usage

일본 연애 문화의 **고백의 정석**. 2-3개월 친밀해진 후, in-person.

## Variations

- **好きです。付き合ってください。** — 가장 격식
- **好きです。** — 강한 호감
- **俺の彼女になってください** — 남성 → 여성, 캐주얼
- **俺のことが好きか?** — 매우 캐주얼 (드라마틱)

## Cultural Notes

**이 표현의 문화적 무게**:
서양의 "Will you be my girlfriend?"와 비슷하지만 **더 격식적**.
일본에서는 告白 자체가 큰 이벤트로 인식.

**위치 선택**도 중요**: 공원, 카페, 해변, 옥상 등 분위기 있는 곳.
밤이 더 로맨틱.

**응답**:
- 긍정: 「私も好きです」 (나도 좋아해)
- 보류: 「考えさせて」 (생각하게 해줘)
- 거절: 「ごめんなさい」 (미안)

japanese-dating-culture.md.title: Japanese Dating Culture
japanese-dating-culture.md.body:
# Japanese Dating Culture

**Type:** 文化ノート, 恋愛
**地域:** 日本 (Japan)

## Overview

일본 데이팅 문화는 **间接(間接)·儀礼(儀礼)·段階(段階)** 중심. 감정 표현은 段階적으로 진행되며, 敬語(경어)에서 タメ口(반말)로의 전환이 관계 진전의 핵심 신호. 告白(고백)은 **공식 이벤트**로, 명확한 의례.

## Setting

A first date in Japan: **カフェ, 動物園, 水族館, 映画, 散歩(산책)**. Often daytime (昼) for first date; evening for established couples. Quiet, conversational activities preferred over loud bars.

## Roles

- **告白する人 (kokuhaku suru hito)** — Confessor (traditionally male, but increasingly either)
- **告白される人 (kokuhaku sareru hito)** — Confessee
- **彼氏 / 彼女 (kareshita / kanojo)** — Boyfriend / girlfriend (after confession)
- **気になる人 (ki ni naru hito)** — "Person of interest" (pre-confession)

## Scenario: Typical First Date → Confession

1. **友達 (Tomodachi)** — Friends, met through school/work/hobby
2. **気になる / 片思い (Ki ni naru / Kataomoi)** — One or both feel attraction
3. **何回かデート (Nankai ka deeto)** — Several dates, group + 1-on-1
4. **告白 (Kokuhaku)** — Confession, often in person, often a "dramatic" moment
5. **付き合う (Tsukiau)** — Officially dating
6. **恋人 (Koibito)** — Established couple

## Cultural Insight

### 告白 (Kokuhaku) 文化

일본에서 告白 = 공식 이벤트. 서양의 "DTR" (Define The Relationship)보다 더 의례적이고 무게감 있음.

**典型的な流れ**:
1. 友達 (친구)
2. 気になる / 片思い (썸/짝사랑)
3. 何回かデート (몇 번 데이트)
4. 告白 (고백, in-person)
5. 付き合う (사귀다)
6. 恋人 (연인)

### 敬語 → タメ口 シフト

**가장 중요한 관계성 전환점**. 연인 사이의 가장 큰 신호 중 하나.

- 그 전: 「○○さん、好きです」
- 후: 「○○ちゃん、好き」

이 시점이 자연스럽게 정해지지 않으면 관계 진행이 막힐 수 있음.

### 脈あり vs 脈なし シグナル

**脈あり (myaku-ari) — 관심이 있는 신호**:
- 자주 연락 (먼저)
- 2人だけの時間 を求める
- ボディタッチ OK (어깨, 팔)
- 食事에 おごろうとする (Z세대: 割り勘 OK)
- 目を見て話す

**脈なし (myaku-nashi) — 관심 없는 신호**:
- 既読スルー (kidoku suru) — LINE 읽고 답장 안 함
- 短返信 (짧은 답장)
- グループに ばかり混ぜようとする
- 視線合わない

### 割り勘 vs おごり

**世代 차이**:
- 30代 이상: 男性 おごり 일반적
- Z世代: 割り勘 (각자 내기) 主流

**판단 기준**: 연인끼리 처음엔 おごり 가능. Z세대는 最初から 割り勘 가능.

### 個人空間 と 距離感

- 初対面: 1m 이상
- 友達: 50cm-1m
- 恋人: 30-50cm (터치 가능)
- 身体接触: 一般的に 서양보다 적음

### 季節と文化

- **クリスマス**: 恋人 일(day). 커플 행위, イルミネーション.
- **バレンタイン (2/14)**: 여성이 남성에게 チョコ. ホワイトデー (3/14): 남성이 답례.
- **お花見 (4월)**: 벚꽃 구경 데이트.
- **夏祭り (8월)**: 전통 축제 데이트.

### 既婚 / 同棲

- 同棲: 점차 보편화 (20-30대)
- 婚前 동거: 一般的

## Useful Phrases

- [[好き]] — I like you
- [[綺麗]] — beautiful
- [[可愛い]] — cute
- [[優しい]] — kind
- [[面白い]] — interesting/funny
- [[デート]] — date
- [[告白]] — confession
- [[付き合う]] — to date
- [[恋人]] — lover
- 好きです (suki desu) — I like you
- ありがとう (arigatou) — thank you
- おはよう / おやすみ — good morning / good night (intimacy signal)

## What NOT to Do

- ❌ **ボディタッチ 너무 일찍** — 부담
- ❌ **LINE 너무 자주** — 부담
- ❌ **告白 너무 일찍** — 부담
- ❌ **敬語 → タメ口 너무 늦게** — 관계 막힘
- ❌ **既読スルー 続ける** — 끝
- ❌ **人前で大声で愛情表現** — 매우 부적절
- ❌ **食事のマナー 무시** (음료, 이쑤시개 등) — 일본에서 매우 부정적

## Body Language

| Gesture | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 目を見て話す (eye contact while talking) | 脈あり (interested) |
| 髪を触る (touching hair) | 脈あり (flirting) |
| ボディタッチ (light touch on arm) | 脈あり |
| 足を組む (crossing legs toward you) | 脈あり |
| 腕を組む (crossed arms) | 防御的 / 緊張 |
| 視線を逸らす (looking away) | 緊張 / 脈なし |
| ため口 (casual speech) | 親密 (intimate) — 関係進行中 |

## Cross-Reference

- Compare with: [[english-dating-culture]], [[spanish-dating-culture]], [[korean-dating-culture]]
- See also: [[好き]], [[告白]], [[付き合う]], [[恋人]]

## 一般的な失敗

- **ボディタッチ 너무 일찍** — 부담
- **LINE 너무 자주** — 부담
- **告白 너무 일찍** — 부담
- **敬語 → タメ口 너무 늦게** — 관계 막힘
- **既読スルー 続ける** — 끝

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-jp]] — 메인 소스
- 花より男子, 失恋ショコラティエ, ロングバケーション
- 京都日本語学校 — 恋愛表現 シラバス
- NHK 日本語講座
- Z世代 恋愛トレンド レポート (2023)

好き.md.title: 好き
好き.md.body:
# 好き

**Part of Speech:** 形容詞 (な-adjective) / 名詞 (noun)
**Level:** N5

**Definition:** To like; affection. (Romaji: **suki**) Used for people, things, activities, food, ideas.

**読み方:** すき (suki)

**Etymology:** 好 (this kanji alone) + き (kun-reading). 好 = "woman (女) + child (子)" = preference. Ancient Chinese character meaning "good, prefer."

**Pronunciation:**
- 読み方: すき (suki)
- Pitch: 頭高 (L-H) — す↓き↑  / alternatively H-L (acceptable both)
- Type: な-adjective (changes to 好きな, 好きに, 好きだ)

**Memory Tip:** "**S**tandard **U**niversal **K**ind **I**nterest" — SU-KI. Whatever you **suki** = you want more of it. 4 letters = simple word.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ 「好きの人」 → 「好きな人」(na-adj needs な before noun)
- ❌ 「好きありません」 → 「好きじゃありません」or 「好きではありません」(negative な-adj)
- ❌ 「あなたのこと好き」 → 「あなたのことが好き」(が required with のこと = "you" emphasis)
- ❌ 「コーヒーが好きじゃない」→ OK grammatically, but 「好きじゃないです」more natural
- ✅ 「○○が好きです」(standard polite)
- ✅ 「好きになります」(will come to like — process)
- ✅ 「好きになっちゃった」(ended up liking — casual, often used in romance)
- Note: 「好き」to a friend/stranger feels like a **confession** (告白). Be careful!

**Register:** standard polite. Universally appropriate. Also casual in casual contexts.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 200 JP words)

**Visual:**
```
   好 = 女 (woman) + 子 (child)
       "woman prefers child" = preference, liking
   ─────────
   好き = preference + abstract ending = to like
```

## Examples

- 「あなたが好きです」 — I like you (full romantic confession, polite)
- 「○○が好きです」 — I like ___
- 「好きになっちゃった」 — I ended up liking (you) — casual
- 「コーヒーが好きです」 — I like coffee
- 「好きだよ」 — I like you (casual, intimate)
- 「日本料理が好きになりました」 — I've come to like Japanese food

## Mini-Dialogue (Confession)

```
A: あのさ... 話したいことがあるんだけど。   ← 저기... 할 말 있어.
B: うん、何？   ← 응, 뭔데?
A: ずっと前から、あなたのことが好きだった。   ← 오래 전부터, 너를 좋아했어.
B: えっ... 私も。   ← 뭐... 나도.
A: 本当？ 嬉しい。   ← 정말? 기뻐.
B: (笑) よかった。   ← (웃음) 다행이다.
```

## Variations

- 好き(すき) — basic like
- 大好き(だいすき) — love, really like
- 愛してる(あいしてる) — love you (rare, very deep)
- 好感(こうかん) — goodwill (formal)
- 好む(このむ) — to prefer, like (verb, formal)

## Related Terms

- [[嫌い]] — dislike
- [[愛する]] — love (heavy, formal)
- [[告白]] — confession
- [[可愛い]] — cute
- [[綺麗]] — beautiful
- [[恋人]] — lover
- [[彼氏]] / [[彼女]] — boyfriend/girlfriend
- [[付き合う]] — to date, go out with

## Cultural Notes

- **「好きです」= 告白의 표준 표현**. 일본에서는 "I love you"보다 "好きです"를 더 자주 사용 (일본 문화에서 愛する는 매우 무거움).
- **段階 (stages of affection)**:
  - 好き — 좋아함 (호감)
  - 大好き — 매우 좋아함 (강한 호감)
  - 愛してる — 사랑해 (rare, deep, married couples)
  - 愛しています — I love you (very formal, often translated as "I love you" in songs)
- **카드를 게임화**: "Confession" 단계의 단어 — 발음이 발라드 (ballad) 가사에 자주 등장.
- **"告白"는 거절할 수 있는 분위기** — 일본에서는 상대에게 부담을 주지 않는 매너 (호감을 표현하되 답을 강요하지 않음).
- **타이밍**: 일본 데이팅에서 好き表白은 2-3개월 이상 교제 후가 일반적. 너무 빠르면 부담.

guapo.md.title: guapo
guapo.md.body:
# guapo

**Part of Speech:** adjetivo (adjective)
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** Handsome (for men); attractive (for women in Spain); cool/stylish (for things/situations). The most common Spanish compliment for appearance.

**Gender Agreement:**
- Masculino: guapo / guapos (pl.) / guapísimo (superlative)
- Femenino: guapa / guapas (pl.) / guapísima (superlative)

**Etymology:** Possibly from Gothic "waps" (bold, brave). The "p" is unusual in Spanish — most words don't have it. The shift: brave → bold-looking → handsome (soldierly virtue became aesthetic).

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /ˈɡwapo/ (masc.) /ˈɡwapa/ (fem.)
- Syllables: gua-po / gua-pa
- Stress: 1st syllable (GUA-po)
- The "gu" is pronounced /gw/ (not /g/)

**Memory Tip:** "**GUA**rdian **PO**werful" — a GUA-po (guapo) person has Guardian Power = strong, capable, attractive. Or: the word sounds slightly rebellious (the "p"!), so it suits a confident man.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "Guapa" for a man → wrong gender; use **guapo**
- ❌ "Es guapo" for a woman → "**Es guapa**" (gender agreement required)
- ❌ "Los chicos son guapo" → "**Los chicos son guapos**" (pl. agreement)
- ❌ "Muy guapo" (always) → "**Está muy guapo hoy**" (está = looks, for temporary)
- ❌ "Guapo" for a child → avoid (sounds like a comment on adult appearance)
- ✅ "Es muy guapo" (He's very handsome — permanent quality)
- ✅ "Qué guapa estás hoy" (You look so pretty today — temporary)
- ✅ "Mi novio es guapísimo" (My boyfriend is super handsome)
- ✅ "Qué día más guapo" (What a nice day) — Spain usage
- Note: "Guapo" is **also used for women in Spain** but Latin American Spanish typically reserves "guapa" for women. "Bonita" is more universal for women.

**Register:** casual to semi-formal. Universally appropriate. Direct.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 ES words)

**Visual:**
```
   G-U-A-P-O
       ─┬─
       "p" = bold, distinctive
   ─────────
   guapo = bold, attractive
```

## Examples

- "Es muy guapo" — He's very handsome
- "Qué guapa estás hoy" — You look so pretty today
- "Mi novio es guapísimo" — My boyfriend is super handsome
- "El coche es muy guapo" — The car is really cool
- "Qué guapo de ti" — How kind of you (alternative use)
- "¡Guapo, ven aquí!" — Handsome, come here! (Spain, casual, sometimes ironic)

## Mini-Dialogue (Compliment)

```
A: ¿Conoces a mi hermano?   ← 내 남동생 알아?
B: ¿El de la foto?   ← 사진에 있는 사람?
A: Sí. Es guapo, ¿no?   ← 응. 잘생겼지, 않아?
B: Mucho. Tiene unos ojos preciosos.   ← 매우. 눈이 정말 예뻐.
A: Se lo digo.   ← 말해줄게.
B: (riéndose) Seguro que le alegra el día.   ← (웃으며) 분명 기분 좋을 거야.
```

## Variations by Region

- **Spain**: "guapo" for men, "guapa" for women — both common
- **Mexico**: "guapo" for men, "guapa/bonita/linda" for women
- **Argentina**: "lindo/guapo" for men, "linda/guapa" for women
- **Colombia**: "guapo/guapa" both common
- **Chile**: "guapo/guapa" common
- **Caribbean**: "guapo" can also mean "angry" (different meaning!)

## Related Terms

- [[bonita]] — pretty (fem., more general)
- [[precioso]] — gorgeous (more intense)
- [[hermoso]] — beautiful (literary)
- [[lindo]] / [[linda]] — pretty/cute (LatAm preferred)
- [[atractivo]] — attractive (neutral)
- [[encantador]] — charming
- [[atractivo]] — attractive (gender-neutral)
- [[apuesto]] — handsome (literary)

## Cultural Notes

- **Género agreement**:
  - Masculino: guapo / guapísimo
  - Femenino: guapa / guapísima
- **Uso apropiado**:
  - Hombres → "guapo"
  - Mujeres → "guapa" o "bonita"
  - Objetos: "guapo" (casual) / "bonito"
- **Cumplido muy común y bienvenido en culturas hispanas**. Más directo que en inglés.
- **"Piropo"** (street compliment) tradition — Spanish-speaking cultures more direct with appearance compliments than English. "Guapo" is the standard opener.
- **받는 쪽**: 보통 자연스럽게 받아들임. "Gracias" + smile is the norm. Rejecting harshly is considered rude.
- **드라마/영화**: "Guapo/a"는 캐릭터 묘사의 기본 — 잘생긴/예쁜.
- **이중 의미**: 일부 지역 (Caribbean)에서는 "guapo" = 화난 (angry). 문맥 주의.
- **별명**: 친구 사이에서 "guapa" (여자) / "guapo" (남자) 호감 표현으로 사용.

## Sources

- [[spanish-dating-culture]] — Piropos
- [[bonita]] — feminine alternative
- Aula Internacional 2 (A2)
- Diccionario de la lengua española (DLE)
- Real Academia Española (RAE)

bonita.md.title: bonita
bonita.md.body:
# bonita

**Part of Speech:** adjetivo (adjective)
**Level:** A2

**Definition:** Pretty; nice; lovely. Used for women, things, ideas. Less intense than "hermosa" or "preciosa."

**Género:** bonito (masc., sing.) / bonita (fem., sing.) / bonitos (masc., pl.) / bonitas (fem., pl.)

**Etymology:** From Latin "bonus" (good). "Bono" was medieval Italian → Spanish "bueno" (good) + diminutive "-ito" = "kind of good" = "nice, pretty." Path: Latin bonus → Italian bono → Spanish bueno/-ito.

**Pronunciation:**
- IPA: /boˈnita/ (fem.) /boˈnito/ (masc.)
- Syllables: bo-ni-ta
- Stress: 2nd syllable (bo-NI-ta)
- Tilde: No accent marks; stress is regular

**Memory Tip:** "**BONI**ta" sounds like "**bonus**" in Latin = "good extra." A bonus good = "more than good" = pretty! Or: "**BO**nus **NI**ce **TA**sty" = bonita.

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "Bonito" for a woman → WRONG gender; use **bonita** (or bonita for fem. things too)
- ❌ "La mujer es bonito" → "La mujer es **bonita**" (must agree with feminine noun)
- ❌ "Bonita" for a man → use **guapo** (handsome)
- ❌ "Bonita" alone (without noun) → vague; specify: "bonita chica" / "bonita sonrisa" / "bonita casa"
- ❌ Overusing "muy bonita" → sounds generic; be specific ("bonitos ojos" = nice eyes)
- ✅ "Es muy bonita" (She's very pretty — natural)
- ✅ "Qué bonita sonrisa" (What a pretty smile)
- ✅ "La casa es bonita" (The house is nice)
- ✅ "Bonitísima" (super pretty — informal intensifier)
- Note: "Bonita" can also mean "nice/pleasant" (e.g., "una bonita canción" = a nice song), not just pretty.

**Register:** casual to semi-formal. Universally appropriate. Direct but not strong.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 500 ES words)

**Visual:**
```
   BONITA
   └──┬──┘
   bueno (good) + -ita (diminutive)
   = "good little thing" = pretty
```

## Examples

- "Es muy bonita" — She's very pretty
- "Qué bonita sonrisa" — What a beautiful smile
- "La casa es bonita" — The house is nice
- "Bonita ciudad" — Nice city (feminine)
- "El paisaje es bonito" — The scenery is nice (masculine)
- "Qué día más bonito" — What a beautiful day
- "Eres bonita" — You're pretty (direct)

## Mini-Dialogue (Compliment)

```
A: Me gusta tu pelo hoy.   ← 오늘 네 머리카락이 마음에 들어.
B: ¿En serio? ¡Gracias!   ← 정말? 고마워!
A: Estás muy bonita.   ← 정말 예쁘다.
B: (sonríe) Eres muy amable.   ← (미소) 정말 친절하다.
A: Solo digo la verdad.   ← 사실 그대로 말한 거야.
```

## Variations by Region

- **España**: "bonita" common
- **México**: "bonita" + "guapa" common, "linda" very common
- **Argentina**: "linda" preferred, "bonita" somewhat old-fashioned
- **Colombia**: "bonita" + "linda" + "hermosa" all common

## Related Terms

- [[guapo]] — handsome (for men); also "guapa" for attractive women in Spain
- [[precioso]] — gorgeous (more intense)
- [[hermoso]] — beautiful (literary, more intense)
- [[lindo]] / [[linda]] — pretty, cute (LatAm preferred)
- [[mono]] / [[mona]] — cute (Spain)
- [[bello]] / [[bella]] — beautiful (formal/literary)
- [[agradable]] — pleasant (more general)
- [[encantador]] — charming

## Cultural Notes

- **Cumplido estándar** para mujeres. **Aceptable y bienvenido** en contextos sociales normales — más directo que en inglés.
- **Intensificadores**:
  - Bonita → Bonitísima (informal)
  - Bonita → Muy bonita
  - Bonita → Qué bonita (exclamatory)
- **Comparar con inglés**:
  - "Cute" (inglés) ≈ "Linda" / "Monita" (español)
  - "Beautiful" (inglés) ≈ "Hermosa" / "Preciosa" (español)
  - "Pretty" (inglés) ≈ "Bonita" (español)
- **"Piropo"** (Spanish street compliment) tradition — direct compliments more common than in English. "Bonita" is one of the safest.
- **받는 쪽**: 보통 감사하게 받아들임. "Gracias" + smile is the natural response.
- **남자에게 사용**: 거의 안 함. **guapo** 또는 **atractivo** 사용.

## Sources

- [[spanish-dating-culture]] — Piropos, cumplidos
- [[guapo]] — comparison
- Aula Internacional 2 (A2)
- Diccionario de la lengua española (DLE)

# me-gustas-mucho

**Part of Speech:** expresión (confesión)
**Definition:** "난 너를 많이 좋아해" — Strong expression of attraction.

**Forma:** Me gustas mucho.

## Usage

호감의 강한 표현. "Me gustas" (약) → "Me gustas mucho" (강) → "Te quiero" (격식).

## Variaciones

- **Me gustas.** — 기본 호감
- **Me gustas mucho.** — 강한 호감
- **Me encantas.** — 더 강한 ("you enchant me")
- **Estoy enamorado/a de ti.** — 사랑에 빠짐

## Cultural Notes

**Construcción "gustar"**:
- "Me gustas" — you please me = I like you
- 문법적으로는 역설이지만 자연스러움

**감정 단계**:
1. Me gustas (좋아해)
2. Me gustas mucho (많이 좋아해)
3. Me encantas (매혹당해)
4. Te quiero (사랑해 — 약)
5. Te amo (사랑해 — 강)

**Contextos**: 진지한 관계 진전을 암시. 너무 일찍 사용하면 부담.

## Related Expressions

- [[estoy-enamorado]] — 더 격식
- [[te-amo]] — 최강
- [[encantado-de-conocerte]] — 첫 인사

## Sources

- Prisma B2

- **Me he enamorado de ti.** — 완료형 강조
- **Estoy loco/a por ti.** — 매우 강한 ("I'm crazy about you")

## Cultural Notes

**"Enamorado" vs "Te quiero" vs "Te amo"**:
- **Enamorado**: 감정 상태 ("I'm in love with you")
- **Te quiero**: 가벼운 사랑 ("I love you")
- **Te amo**: 깊은 사랑 ("I love you deeply")

**응답**:
- 긍정: "Yo también estoy enamorado/a de ti"
- 보류: "Necesito tiempo para pensarlo"
- 거절: "Lo siento, pero no siento lo mismo"

espana-vs-latinoamerica-registro.md.title: España vs. Latinoamérica - registro coloquial
espana-vs-latinoamerica-registro.md.body:
# España vs. Latinoamérica - registro coloquial

**Overview:** El español peninsular y el de Latinoamérica divergen notablemente en registro coloquial. Un hispanohablante peninsular y uno mexicano o argentino pueden entenderse perfectamente y, sin embargo, no reconocerse mutuamente como "del mismo dialecto" en conversación informal.

## Key Points

- **Muletillas** son el primer marcador de dialecto: *"vale"*, *"ostras"* (España) vs. *"órale"*, *"padrísimo"* (México) vs. *"dale"*, *"che"* (Argentina)
- **Voseo** solo en Argentina, Uruguay, parte de Centroamérica y Chile (en voseo mixto). España usa **tú** universal.
- **"Guapo"** halaga en España, puede sonar anticuado en México, significa **matón** en Cuba
- **Vocabulario de fiesta**: *botellón* (España), *junta/peda* (México), *previa* (Argentina/Uruguay)
- **Sustantivos coloquiales** para trabajar: *currar* (España), *chambear* (México), *laburar* (Argentina)

## Language Connections

- [[siesta]] - práctica en declive en ciudades, viva en zonas rurales
- [[tapas]] - rito social exclusivamente peninsular
- [[guapo]] - falso amigo cultural crítico
- [[mola]] - inequívocamente español peninsular
- [[currar]] - marca de origen
- [[botellon]] - fenómeno social español
- [[calor-pegajoso]] - más común en zonas costeras y tropicales

## Tabla rápida

| Concepto         | España          | México              | Argentina           |
| ---------------- | --------------- | ------------------- | ------------------- |
| Trabajar         | currar          | chambear            | laburar             |
| Guay/mola        | mola, guay      | chido, padre        | copado, piola       |
| Fiesta callejera | botellón        | peda, junta         | previa, joda        |
| De acuerdo       | vale            | sale, órale         | dale, bueno         |
| Llamar a la puerta | llamar, tocar  | tocar               | timbear             |

## Sources

- [[notes-in-spanish-listening-log]]
- [[siesta]]
- [[tapas]]

matriarca.md.title: matriarca
matriarca.md.body:
# matriarca

**Part of Speech:** sustantivo femenino

**Definition:** Mujer que ejerce el poder o la autoridad principal en una familia o grupo. Madre que gobierna con mano firme.

**Etymology:** Del latín *mater* (madre) + griego *archē* (mando, gobierno).

## Examples

- *Su madre, doña Carmen, era la **matriarca** de la familia De la Garza.*
- *La **matriarca** decidió que el menor no se casaría.*
- *Es una **matriarca** respetada por todos sus hijos y nietos.*

## Related Terms

- [[patriarca]] - versión masculina
- [[cabeza-de-familia]] - neutro
- [[matriarcado]] - sistema social

## Cultural Notes

En Latinoamérica rural del siglo XX, la **matriarca** tenía poder absoluto sobre la vida de las hijas solteras, especialmente en lo referente al matrimonio. **Mama Elena** en *Como agua para chocolate* es el arquetipo: su autoridad es ley indiscutible, respaldada por la "tradición sagrada". El conflicto novela-matriarca es uno de los grandes temas de la literatura latinoamericana.

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]
- [[mexico-patriarcado-tradicion]]

chapparon.md.title: chaparrón
chapparon.md.body:
# chaparrón

**Part of Speech:** sustantivo masculino

**Definition:** Lluvia fuerte y breve, usualmente con tormenta. Aguacero.

**Etymology:** Del vasco *txaparrón* o del antiguo *chapuz* (chapuzón). Posible influencia del euskera.

## Examples

- *Viene un **chaparrón**, mejor esperar un rato.*
- *Cayó un **chaparrón** tremendo y nos empapamos.*
- *El **chaparrón** solo duró diez minutos.*

## Related Terms

- [[aguacero]] - lluvia intensa
- [[tormenta]] - con aparato eléctrico
- [[llovizna]] - lluvia fina
- [[chaparrón-vespertino]] - el típico de verano

## Cultural Notes

El **chaparrón** es la lluvia típica de verano en España: cae con fuerza, dura poco, y refresca. En Madrid, después del **chaparrón** vespertino suele salir el sol y aparecer el arco iris. Culturalmente, el **chaparrón** es welcome break del calor. En Latinoamérica, **chaparrón** también se usa pero es más común en zonas andinas y centrales.

## Sources

- [[notes-in-spanish-planes-de-verano]]

subirse-al-higado.md.body:
# subirse al hígado

**Literal Translation:** ím ao figado (subir al hígado)

**Meaning:** Enojarse mucho, irritarse profundamente. La cólera sube desde el estómago hasta la cabeza pero se siente como si se aposentara en el hígado.

**Usage Context:** Coloquial, en España y buena parte de Latinoamérica. Expresión de disgusto profundo, no de furia explosiva.

## Examples

- *A los hombres se les **subieron al hígado** las suspicacias y empezaron a rezongar.*
- *Se me **subió al hígado** que no me agradecieran.*
- *Esas críticas se le **subieron al hígado**.*

## Cultural Background

Metáfora del cuerpo como contenedor de emociones. El hígado, junto con el corazón y el pecho, es sede clásica de la ira y el rencor en la cultura hispana ("me da en el hígado", "arderle el hígado"). García Márquez elige "subirse" (acción ascendente, contenido acumulado) frente a "arder" (acción presente) para sugerir enojo que se va gestando.

## Similar Expressions

- [[arder-el-higado]] - forma más común
- [[no-poder-tragar]] - enojo contenido
- [[estar-hasta-las-ancas]] - estar harto (Argentina)

## Sources

- [[el-ahogado-mas-hermoso-del-mundo]]

pueblo-costero-funeral.md.title: pueblo costero y funeral
pueblo-costero-funeral.md.body:
# pueblo costero y funeral

**Overview:** En pueblos pequeños de costa (Caribe, Galicia, costas del Pacífico americano), la muerte de un desconocido activa rituales comunitarios. El cuerpo sin dolientes se integra al tejido social del pueblo antes de su destino final.

## Key Points

- El mar es cementerio y madre a la vez
- El entierro de náufragos suele ser comunitario, sin亲人
- Las mujeres lideran el cuidado del cuerpo; los hombres, el destino final
- La identificación por nombre propio es rito de paso: "ya es uno de los nuestros"
- El funeral se convierte en transformación del pueblo entero

## Language Connections

- [[acantilado]] - el punto de devolución al mar
- [[angarillas]] - camilla improvisada con restos marinos
- [[bobo]] - apelativo paradójico del difunto
- [[desvalido]] - el muerto que se humaniza por el duelo
- [[quedar-varado]] - el cuerpo que se detiene antes de su último viaje
- [[devolverlo-huerfano-a-las-aguas]] - rito de despedida

## Fuentes

- García Márquez, *El ahogado más hermoso del mundo* (1968)
- Juan Rulfo, *Pedro Páramo* - otra tradición funeraria latinoamericana
- Costumbres gallegas de entierro marítimo de náufragos
- Tradiciones pesqueras de Centroamérica y Caribe

## Sources

- [[el-ahogado-mas-hermoso-del-mundo]]
- [[realismo-magico-marquez]]

recetario-como-estructura.md.title: estructura de recetario (Esquivel)
recetario-como-estructura.md.body:
# estructura de recetario (Esquivel)

**Overview:** *Como agua para chocolate* es la primera novela de estructura **recetario** en la literatura hispanoamericana. Cada capítulo se abre con una receta, narrada en segunda persona (tú), que se entrelaza con la ficción. La novela es, literalmente, un libro de cocina + novela a la vez.

## Key Points

- **12 capítulos = 12 meses = 12 recetas**: enero a diciembre
- **Las recetas son segunda persona**: "Pela la cebolla, llora con ella..."
- **Las recetas son narrativas, no solo instructivas**: cuentan el estado emocional
- **Los ingredientes funcionan como símbolos**: cebolla = llanto, masa = tradición, chiles = pasión
- **El lector se convierte en cocinero**: puede seguir las recetas mientras lee
- **Decisión formal radical**: equiparar literatura con cocina

## Language Connections

- [[receta]] - la unidad básica
- [[cebolla]] - ingrediente simbólico
- [[masa]] - base de la cocina mexicana
- [[a-fuego-lento]] - técnica y tiempo
- [[ingredientes]] - lo que se mezcla

## Influencia

- Novela de Esquivel (1989) → éxito internacional
- **Frontera** de la escritora **Gloria Anzaldúa** (1987): teoría de frontera (mezcla de géneros) es coetánea
- **Chick lit** posterior y *food writing* deben algo a Esquivel
- La novela anticipa el interés cultural por la **cocina como patrimonio inmaterial** (UNESCO, s. XXI)

## Sources

- [[como-agua-para-chocolate-cap1]]
- [[receta]]
- [[cocina-espacio-femenino]]

spanish-dating-culture.md.title: Spanish Dating Culture
spanish-dating-culture.md.body:
# Spanish Dating Culture

**Type:** Notas culturales, romance
**Región:** España / Latinoamérica

## Overview

스페인어권 데이팅 문화는 **직접(direct)·가족 지향(family-oriented)·감정 표현 풍부(emotionally expressive)**. Piropo(길거리 칭찬)는 자연스럽고 환영받음. 가족과友朋友的 교류가 매우 중요. テンポ는 영어권보다 빠르고, 한국/일본보다 약간 느린 중간.

## Setting

A first date in Spain: **tapas bar, café, walk in park, or cinema**. Evening (20-22h is common in Spain; 19-21h in LatAm). Two people meeting with romantic intent. Casual to smart-casual dress.

## Roles

- **El que invita** — Person who proposes the date
- **El invitado** — Person who accepts
- **Pareja** — Couple (after formal dating starts)
- **Novio / Novia** — Boyfriend / girlfriend

## Scenario: First Date in Spain

1. **Propuesta** — "¿Te gustaría cenar el viernes?" (Would you like to have dinner Friday?)
2. **Confirmación** — Confirmar hora, lugar
3. **Llegada** —Llegar 5-10 min tarde es aceptable en España (no en México)
4. **Saludo** — 1 beso en la mejilla (Spain) o 2 (LatAm) al encontrarse
5. **Conversación** — Personal pero no demasiado íntima al principio
6. **Comida/bebida** — Tapas, vino, cerveza
7. **La cuenta** — "¿Invitamos a medias?" o "Yo invito"
8. **Caminar después** — Paseo, conversación extendida
9. **Despedida** — Beso(s) en la mejilla + propuesta de verse de nuevo
10. **Mensaje** — Mensaje de texto al llegar a casa

## Cultural Insight

### Cumplidos Directos (Piropos)

스페인어권에서 **direct compliments**는 자연스럽고 환영받음. 영어권보다 훨씬 직설적.

**Ejemplos apropiados**:
- "Qué guapa estás hoy" — You look pretty today
- "Me encanta tu sonrisa" — I love your smile
- "Tienes unos ojos preciosos" — You have beautiful eyes
- "Qué vestido más bonito" — What a pretty dress

**Línea roja**: Contextos inapropiados (calle,陌生人 acosando). Piropo a desconocidos en la calle = acoso.

### Beso en la Mejilla

**Saludo estándar** entre conocidos. Número varía:
- **España**: 1 beso (sólo mujeres entre sí o mixto)
- **Latinoamérica**: 2 besos (más universal)
- **Hombres entre sí**: apretón de manos (LatAm) o abrazo (algunos)

### Vosotros vs Ustedes

**Distinción importante**:
- **España**: "vosotros" (informal plural) / "ustedes" (formal)
- **Latinoamérica**: sólo "ustedes" para todos los casos
- Coqueteo siempre usa **tú** (nunca usted)

### "Te Quiero" vs "Te Amo"

**Escala de amor**:
- Me gustas (gusto) — 호감
- Te quiero (querer) — 가벼운 사랑 (1-3 meses)
- Te amo (amar) — 깊은 사랑 (después)
- Te adoro (adorar) — 가장 강함

**Regla**: "Te quiero" en meses 1-3, "Te amo" después.

### Contacto Visual

- **Prolongado = interés**. Bajar la mirada = modestia o desinterés.
- **Ligeramente más cerca que en inglés** — 30-50cm en conversación.

### Pago de la Cuenta

**Generación**:
- Antiguos: hombre paga
- Millennials/Gen Z: 50/50 (más común) o "invita el que propuso"
- **Frase útil**: "¿Invitamos a medias?" (Shall we split?)

### Personalidad > Apariencia

En Latinoamérica, **la familia y la conexión emocional** son importantes. Solo appearance-based relationships menos valoradas que en otras culturas.

### Consentimiento

**Explícito es la norma**:
- "¿Puedo darte un beso?" (Can I kiss you?)
- "¿Te puedo coger la mano?" (Can I hold your hand?)

No preguntar = red flag.

## Useful Phrases

- [[bonita]] — pretty
- [[guapo]] — handsome
- [[amar]] — to love (deep)
- [[querer]] — to love (lighter)
- [[beso]] — kiss
- [[cita]] — date
- [[te-quiero]] — I love you (lighter)
- [[te-amo]] — I love you (deeper)
- Me gustas — I like you
- Encantado/a — Nice to meet you
- ¿Quieres... ? — Do you want... ?
- Te invito — My treat (I'll pay)

## What NOT to Do

- ❌ **Piropo a desconocidos en la calle** — acoso
- ❌ **"Te amo" en la primera cita** — muy fuerte
- ❌ **Hablar mal de su familia** — familia es sagrada
- ❌ **Llegar muy tarde a propósito** — falta de respeto
- ❌ **Ser demasiado formal al principio** — España/LatAm prefieren calidez
- ❌ **Insistir después de un "no"** — red flag
- ❌ **Confundir "sí" con "no"** — ser claro

## Body Language

| Gesture | Meaning |
|---|---|
| Contacto visual prolongado | Interesado |
| Sonrisa cálida + ojos | Friendly, possibly flirting |
| 1-2 besos en mejilla al saludar | Estándar |
| Tocar el brazo ligeramente | Coqueteo |
| Acercarse físicamente | Interesado |
| Bajar la mirada con timidez | Modestia, no desinterés |
| Cruzarse de brazos | Defensivo |
| Mirar el teléfono | Desinterés |

## Cross-Reference

- Compare with: [[english-dating-culture]], [[japanese-dating-culture]], [[korean-dating-culture]]
- See also: [[bonita]], [[guapo]], [[te-amo]], [[cita]], [[beso]]

## Citas / Eventos Especiales

- **San Valentín (14 Feb)**: Grande en México, España. Menos en otros países.
- **Quinceañera (15 años)**: México, LatAm — Familiar celebration.
- **Aniversarios**: Mensual commemoration no es tan común como en Corea.

## Diferencias España vs LatAm

- **Horario**: España cena 21-23h. LatAm cena 19-21h.
- **Contacto físico inicial**: LatAm más rápido.
- **Familiaridad**: LatAm más rápido.
- **Lenguaje formal**: España más formal inicialmente.
- **Cumplidos**: España más contenido; LatAm más efusivo.

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-es]] — 메인 소스
- Tres metros sobre el cielo
- Élite (Netflix)
- DELE B1/B2 자료
- RAE Diccionario
- Protocolo social hispanoamericano

realismo-magico-esquivel.md.title: realismo mágico (Esquivel)
realismo-magico-esquivel.md.body:
# realismo mágico (Esquivel)

**Overview:** Laura Esquivel es heredera del realismo mágico de García Márquez, pero su **varita mágica** es la **comida**: a diferencia de otros autores, el prodigio no se manifiesta en apariciones o viajes, sino en que **la comida transmite emociones**. Las lágrimas de Tita al pelar cebollas se mezclan con la masa y los que comen sienten su tristeza.

## Key Points

- **Subversión de la cocina**: la cocina no es solo lugar doméstico, es **tejido narrativo**
- **Emociones comestibles**: cada receta está emocionalmente cargada
- **Recetario como estructura**: 12 capítulos, 12 meses, 12 recetas
- **Feminidad mágica**: Tita, Gertrudis, Nacha - las mujeres tienen el poder
- **Mestizaje cultural**: recetas mexicanas + estructura europea + magia indígena

## Language Connections

- [[cebolla]] - el alimento que evoca lágrimas

annyeonghaseyo.md.title: 안녕하세요
annyeonghaseyo.md.body:
# 안녕하세요

**Part of Speech:** 인사 (greeting, interjection)
**Level:** TOPIK I (1-2)

**Definition:** Standard polite greeting, equivalent to "hello" in English. Used at any time of day in Korean.

**Word Origin:** 한자어 (Sino-Korean) — 안녕 (安寧, peace/safety) + 하 (do/be) + 세 (formal ending base) + 요 (polite ending). Originally meant "are you at peace?"

**Pronunciation:**
- Revised Romanization: annyeonghaseyo
- 読み方 (음): [안녕하세요]
- Syllables: an-nyeong-ha-se-yo (5 syllables)
- Note: Pronounced [안녕하세요] — not [안녕하게요] (some learners make this mistake)
- The ㅎ in 하 is silent in casual speech: [안녕아세요] can be heard

**Memory Tip:** "**ANN**yeong" sounds like "annyeong" (the word itself) — say it like a slight bow. "하세" (ha-se) = "do you" — so literally "are you at peace?" Modern usage = "hello."

**Common Mistakes:**
- ❌ "안녕" alone to a stranger → **too casual**; only with friends/family. "안녕하세요" is the safe form.
- ❌ "안녕하십니까" → very formal, almost archaic; use only in very specific contexts
- ❌ "안녕하세요" bowing not at all → feels insincere; pair with a slight bow (15-30 degrees for casual, 45+ for formal)
- ❌ "안녕히 가세요" (when you, the speaker, are leaving) → "안녕히 가세요" = goodbye to person leaving; "**안녕히 계세요**" = goodbye to person staying
- ✅ "안녕하세요" (Hello — universal)
- ✅ "안녕하세요, 만나서 반갑습니다" (Hello, nice to meet you)
- ✅ "선생님, 안녕하세요!" (Hello, teacher!)
- ✅ "친구에게: 안녕하세요, 잘 지내요?" (To friend: Hello, how are you?)
- Note: Unlike Japanese "こんにちは" (daytime only), Korean 안녕하세요 has **no time restriction** — use 24/7.

**Register:** casual polite (해요체). Universally appropriate.
**Frequency:** ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ (top 30 KR words)

**Speech Levels:**
- Casual intimate (해체): 안녕 (to close friends/family only)
- Casual polite (해요체): 안녕하세요 (universal)
- Formal polite (합쇼체): 안녕하십니까 (very formal, rare)
- Casual text: ㅎㅇ (internet slang)

## Examples

- 안녕하세요, 만나서 반갑습니다. — Hello, nice to meet you.
- 선생님, 안녕하세요! — Hello, teacher!
- 친구에게: 안녕하세요, 잘 지내요? — To a friend: Hello, how are you?
- 손님, 안녕하세요. 뭘 도와드릴까요? — Customer, hello. How can I help? (service)
- 안녕하세요, 김민수입니다. — Hello, this is Kim Min-su. (phone)
- 안녕히 가세요 / 안녕히 계세요 — Goodbye (to leaver / to stayer)

## Mini-Dialogue (First Meeting)

```
A: 안녕하세요, 처음 뵙겠습니다. 김민수입니다.   ← 안녕하세요, 처음 뵙겠습니다. 김민수입니다.
B: 안녕하세요, 만나서 반갑습니다. 박지영입니다.   ← 안녕하세요, 만나서 반갑습니다. 박지영입니다.
A: 잘 부탁드립니다.   ← 잘 부탁드립니다.
B: 네, 잘 부탁드립니다.   ← 네, 잘 부탁드립니다.
A: (악수)   ← (악수)
B: (미소)   ← (미소)
```

## Variations

| Form | Korean | Register | When to use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 안녕 | an-nyeong | casual intimate | Friends, family, juniors (반말) |
| 안녕하세요 | an-nyeong-ha-se-yo | casual polite | Universal default |
| 안녕하십니까 | an-nyeong-ha-sim-ni-kka | very formal | Very rare, archaic-feeling |
| 안녕~ | an-nyeong~ | casual text/friend | Casual messaging |
| ㅎㅇ | (short for 하이/hello) | internet slang | Very casual texting |
| 처음 뵙겠습니다 | cheo-eum bwep-seum-ni-da | formal | First meeting (often paired) |
| 만나서 반갑습니다 | man-na-seo bang-gap-seum-ni-da | formal | First meeting (often paired) |
| 잘 부탁드립니다 | jal bu-tak-deu-lim-ni-da | formal | "Please treat me well" (closing greeting) |

## Related Terms

- [[감사합니다]] — thank you
- [[죄송합니다]] — I'm sorry
- [[반갑습니다]] — glad to meet you
- 잘 지내요 — how are you (response)
- 잘 부탁드립니다 — please treat me well
- 처음 뵙겠습니다 — first time meeting
- 안녕히 가세요 — goodbye (to leaver)
- 안녕히 계세요 — goodbye (to stayer)
- [[친구]] — friend
- [[가족]] — family

## Cultural Notes

- 한국의 일상적 인사. 정중체(합쇼체)로, 아는 사람에게도 처음 만나는 사람에게도 사용 가능. 낮 시간대 구분이 없음 (24시간 사용).
- 일본어のこんにちは와 비교: 일본어는 낮 시간에만 사용하지만 한국어 안녕하세요는 시간 구분 없음.
- **Bowing and handshakes**:
  - **Friends, casual**: slight nod or no bow
  - **Business, formal**: 15-30 degree bow + handshake
  - **Very formal (older person, authority)**: 45+ degree bow
  - **Younger to older**: deeper bow (respect for age hierarchy)
- **Age and hierarchy**: 
  - To older people: deeper bow, more formal speech (합쇼체 with 안녕하십니까)
  - To same age: casual polite (안녕하세요)
  - To younger (juniors): can be more casual
- **Greeting custom**: When entering a room with elders, greet them first (안녕하세요 to each senior person).
- **First meeting ritual**:
  - 안녕하세요 (hello)
  - 처음 뵙겠습니다 (first time meeting — formal) OR 만나서 반갑습니다 (glad to meet you)
  - 잘 부탁드립니다 (please treat me well — closing)
  - This 3-part greeting is standard in business and formal social settings.
- **Cell phone culture**: When answering, "여보세요" (yeo-bo-se-yo) is used, NOT "안녕하세요."

## Sources

- TOPIK 1급 어휘 목록
- 국립국어원 표준국어대사전
- [[korean-dating-culture]] — Greetings, age hierarchy
- [[topik1-starter]]
- KBS 한국어 — 인사 표현

sumimasen-wa-doko-desuka.md.title: sumimasen-wa-doko-desuka (すみません、…はどこですか?)
sumimasen-wa-doko-desuka.md.body:
# sumimasen-wa-doko-desuka (すみません、…はどこですか?)

**Part of Speech:** 表現 (expression)

**Definition:** "실례합니다, ...는 어디인가요?" — 위치/방향 물을 때 쓰는 가장 기본적인 일본어 표현.

**Romaji:** sumimasen, ... wa doko desu ka?

**Formality:** Polite (です/ます체)

## Usage

일본 여행에서 길을 묻거나 사물·장소의 위치를 확인할 때 가장 자주 쓰는 표현.

## Components

- **すみません (sumimasen)** — "excuse me" / "I'm sorry" / "thank you"
- **...は (wa)** — topic particle
- **どこ (doko)** — "where"
- **ですか (desu ka)** — "is it?"

## Examples

- **すみません、駅はどこですか?** — 실례합니다, 역은 어디인가요?
- **すみません、空港はどこですか?** — 실례합니다, 공항은 어디인가요?
- **すみません、美術館はどこですか?** — 실례합니다, 미술관은 어디인가요?

## Variations

- **場所を聞く**: ...はどこですか?
- **行き方を聞く**: ...へはどう行けばいいですか? — 어떻게 가나요?
- **物を聞く**: ...はありますか? — ...가 있나요?

## Related Terms

- [[kuukou]] — 공항
- [[eki]] — 역
- [[chikatetsu]] — 지하철
- [[massugu]] — 직진

## Cultural Notes

すみません은 세 가지 용도로 쓰임:
1. **호출/실례** — "Excuse me"
2. **사과** — "I'm sorry"
3. **감사** — "Thank you" (약식)

한국어의 "저기요" + "감사합니다"를 합친 느낌. 너무 자주 쓰면 과한 뉘앙스가 될 수 있음.

## Sources

- [[first-travel-japan]]
- [[first-travel-korea]]

korean-dating-culture.md.title: Korean Dating Culture
korean-dating-culture.md.body:
# Korean Dating Culture

**Type:** 문화 노트, 恋愛
**지역:** 한국 (대한민국)

## Overview

한국 데이팅 문화는 **단계(段階)·계열(階級)·K-content 영향** 중심. 썸(좁은曖昧함)이라는 독특한 단계, 존댓말→반말 전환의 결정성, 100일 기념일 의례가 특징. K-drama/K-pop이 роман틱 표현에 큰 영향.

## Setting

A first date in Korea: **카페, 식당, 영화, 전시회, 공원**. Daytime preferred for first dates (낮 데이트); evening for established couples. 둘만의 시간(2人だけの時間)을 점점 늘려가는 패턴.

## Roles

- **썸 상대 (some sangdae)** — Person you're in "some" stage with
- **고백하는 사람 (go-baeng-ha-neun sa-ram)** — Confessor
- **사귀는 사람 (sa-gwi-neun sa-ram)** — Person you're dating
- **연인 (yeon-in)** — Lover/partner
- **남자친구 / 여자친구 (nam-ja-chin-gu / yeo-ja-chin-gu)** — Boyfriend/girlfriend (literal: "male friend"/"female friend" but means romantic partner)

## Scenario: Korean Dating Stages

1. **관심 (관심 단계)** — Interest developing, often through mutual friends/work
2. **썸 (Some stage)** — Mutual interest, ambiguous, 1-3 months
3. **고백 (Confession)** — Explicit declaration, often with question
4. **사귀다 (Dating)** — Official couple
5. **연인 (Established couple)** — Long-term, possibly moving toward marriage
6. **동거 / 결혼** — Cohabitation / marriage

## Cultural Insight

### 썸 (Some) 문화

한국만의 독특한 연애 단계. 영어/일본어에 직접 번역 없음.

**단계**:
1. 관심 (interest) — 눈치
2. 썸 (some) — 서로 관심, 모호함
3. 고백 (confession) — 명시
4. 사귀다 (dating) — 공식 연인
5. 연인 (couple)

**썸의 핵심 질문**: "우리 지금 뭐야?" (What are we right now?)

**썸의 특징**:
- 1-3개월 지속
- 양쪽 모두 관심 있지만 명시 안 함
- "관심 있어 보이는데 확인이 안 됨"

### 존댓말 → 반말 전환

**가장 중요한 관계 신호 중 하나**. 연인 사이의 핵심 전환점.

- 전: "○○씨, 오늘 뭐 했어요?" (격식)
- 후: "○○아, 오늘 뭐 했어?" (반말)

**이 전환을 자연스럽게**: 보통 고백 → 사귀기 후 며칠 내.

**주의**: 영원히 존댓말 = "친구로 남고 싶다"의 신호.

### 호칭 (Titles)

**호칭이 관계를 정의**:
- 여자친구 → "오빠" (남자친구) / "자기야"
- 남자친구 → "여보" / "자기" / "이름+아"
- 친한 남자 친구 → "형" / "오빠" (주의: 연인 ❌)
- 친한 여자 친구 → "언니" / "누나"

**애칭 (endearments)**: 자기야, 여보, 내사랑, 왕자님, 공주님, 꿀님

### 100일 (100 Days) 기념일

**한국만의 독특한 문화**: 100일 단위로 기념.

**주요 100일**:
- 100일 — 첫 번째 큰 기념일
- 200일, 300일, 500일
- 1000일 — 매우 큰 milestone

**선물**: 꽃, 케이크, 작은 선물, 손편지.

### 결제 문화

**전통**: 남자친구가 계산.
**Z세대 변화**:
- 더치페이 (Dutch pay) 흔함
- "내가 낼게" / "다음에 네가 내"
- 각자 다른 문화 존중

### 계절 / 기념일

- **발렌타인 (2/14)**: 여자가 남자에게 초콜릿
- **화이트데이 (3/14)**: 남자가 여자에게 사탕
- **빼빼로데이 (11/11)**: 빼빼로 교환
- **기념일**: 100일 단위 + 1주년

### K-Drama / K-Pop 영향

**한국 데이트 문화는 K-content에 영향**:
- "오빠" 호칭 (드라마 영향)
- 썸 문화 (드라마 묘사)
- 100일 기념일
- 이상형 (ideal type) 개념

### Consent

**현대 한국에서도 명시적 동의가 표준**:
- "손 잡아도 돼?" (Can I hold your hand?)
- "키스해도 돼?" (Can I kiss you?)

**질투 표현**: "이 사람 누구야?" "왜 그 사람이랑?" — 한국 연애의 일부로 받아들여짐 (단, 폭언/통제는 NO).

## Useful Phrases

- [[안녕하세요]] — hello
- [[감사합니다]] — thank you
- [[죄송합니다]] — I'm sorry
- [[sarang]] — love
- [[chingu]] — friend (also boyfriend/girlfriend as 남자친구/여자친구)
- 좋아해요 — I like you
- 사랑해 — I love you
- 보고 싶어 — I miss you
- 자기야 — honey/baby
- 여보 — honey (married)
- 고백할게 — I'll confess
- 사귀자 — Let's date

## What NOT to Do

- ❌ **너무 일찍 반말** (친구로 인식될 위험)
- ❌ **너무 일찍 신체 접촉** (불편)
- ❌ **지나친 소유욕/질투** (독)
- ❌ **연락 안 하기** (관심 없는 신호)
- ❌ **100일 / 기념일 무시** (큰 매너 위반)
- ❌ **"우리 사귀자" 너무 일찍** (1-2주 내) — 부담
- ❌ **공개적인 애정 표현 (PDA) 과도** — 부끄럽게 느낄 수 있음
- ❌ **전 애인 비교** — 절대 금기

## Body Language

| Gesture | Meaning |
|---|---|
| 손 잡기 (holding hands) | 관심, 사귀는 중 |
| 팔짱 끼기 (linked arms) | 친밀 |
| 어깨 기대기 (leaning on shoulder) | 편안함 |
| 머리 쓰다듬기 (patting head) | 애정 (남→여) |
| 손등 쓰다듬기 | 부드러운 애정 |
| 눈 마주침 + 미소 | 관심 표현 |
| 시선 회피 | 부담, 쑥스러움 |
| 스마트폰 확인 | 관심 없음 (예의 위반) |

## Cross-Reference

- Compare with: [[english-dating-culture]], [[japanese-dating-culture]], [[spanish-dating-culture]]
- See also: [[sarang]], [[chingu]], [[annyeonghaseyo]]

## 100일 / 기념일 문화 상세

- **100일**: 첫 기념일. 꽃, 케이크, 작은 선물
- **200일**: 100일보다 덜 중요하지만 챙김
- **300일**: 관계 안정화 시점
- **500일**: 중요한 milestone
- **1000일**: 거의 결혼 수준, 큰 선물
- **1주년**: 365일, 더 중요한 milestone
- **기념일 무시는 큰 매너 위반**

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-kr]] — 메인 소스
- 별에서 온 그대, 사랑의 불시착, 응답하라 1988
- TalkToMeInKorean Level 7-9
- 국립국어원 표준국어대사전
- KBS 한국어
- 한국 연애 트렌드 보고서 (2023)

이름.md.title: 이름
이름.md.body:
# 이름

**Part of Speech:** 명사
**Level:** TOPIK 1급

**Definition:** 이름 — A word by which a person is known.

**어원:** 漢語 '이름' (Sino-Korean)

## Examples

- "이름이 뭐예요?" — What is your name?
- "제 이름은 김민수예요" — My name is Kim Min-su
- "이름이 멋있다" — Cool name

## Related Terms

- [[만나서-반가워요]] — nice to meet you
- [[취미]] — hobby
- [[친구]] — friend

## Cultural Notes

**한국 이름 구조**: 성(姓) + 이름. 예: 김민수 = 김(surname) + 민수(given name).

**호칭 (Titles)**: 연인 사이에서 이름 + 호칭이 관계성을 보임:
- 오빠/오빠 — 연인 (남자친구) / 친한 남자 친구
- 형/누나 — 친한 형제/친구 (연인 ❌)
- 언니/언니 — 여자친구 / 친한 여자 친구

**자기소개**: 처음에는 "이○○입니다" (격식), 친해지면 "나는 ○○야" (반말).

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-kr]] — 자기소개
- TOPIK 1급 어휘
- 국립국어원 표준국어대사전

취미.md.title: 취미
취미.md.body:
# 취미

**Part of Speech:** 명사
**Level:** TOPIK 1급

**Definition:** 취미 — Hobby; pastime.

## Examples

- "취미가 뭐야?" — What are your hobbies?
- "제 취미는 영화 보기예요" — My hobby is watching movies
- "취미가 비슷하네" — We have similar hobbies

## Related Terms

- [[이름]] — name
- [[카페]] — café
- [[영화]] — movie

## Cultural Notes

**첫 대화의 표준 주제**. 이름 → 취미 → 계획 순서.

**한국에서 흔한 취미**:
- 영화/드라마 보기
- 운동 (헬스, 러닝, 등산)
- 여행
- 요리
- 게임
- 독서
- 카페 투어
- K-pop / 음악

예쁘다.md.title: 예쁘다
예쁘다.md.body:
# 예쁘다

**Part of Speech:** 형용사
**Level:** TOPIK 2급

**Definition:** 예쁘다 — Pretty; beautiful.

**활용:** 예뻐요 (formal) / 예쁘다 (반말) / 예뻐 (casual)

## Examples

- "예쁘다" — Pretty
- "너 오늘 예쁘다" — You look pretty today
- "예쁜 꽃" — Pretty flower

## Variations

- [[이쁘다]] — 같은 뜻, 구어체 (대부분的年轻人)
- 예쁘다 — 표준
- 아름답다 — formal/문학적
- 귀엽다 — cute

## Related Terms

- [[잘생겼다]] — handsome (men)
- [[귀엽다]] — cute
- [[멋있다]] — cool/stylish

## Cultural Notes

**사용 빈도**: 한국 데이트에서 **가장 흔한 여성 외모 칭찬**.

**주의**: 너무 일찍 또는 너무 자주 사용하면 가벼움. **진심 어린
칭찬으로 사용**해야 효과적.

**반말 사용**: 연인 사이 또는 친한 사이지. 처음에는 "예쁘시네요"
(격식) 사용.

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-kr]] — 칭찬
- TOPIK 2급 어휘
- TalkToMeInKorean Level 7

이쁘다.md.title: 이쁘다
이쁘다.md.body:
# 이쁘다

**Part of Speech:** 형용사
**Level:** TOPIK 2급

**Definition:** 예쁘다 (구어체) — Pretty (colloquial spelling).

## Examples

- "너 이쁘다" — You're pretty
- "이쁜 사람" — Pretty person
- "이쁘게 생겼다" — Born pretty

## Related Terms

- [[예쁘다]] — 표준 표기
- [[귀엽다]] — cute
- [[잘생겼다]] — handsome

## Cultural Notes

**"이쁘다" vs "예쁘다"**:
- 같은 발음, 같은 뜻
- "이쁘다" = 구어체 (구어)
- "예쁘다" = 표준 (글에서)

**Part of Speech:** 형용사
**Level:** TOPIK 2급

**Definition:** 멋있다 — Cool; stylish; impressive.

**활용:** 멋있어요 / 멋있다 / 멋있어 (반말)

## Examples

- "멋있는 사람" — Cool person

**차이점**:

귀엽다.md.body:
# 귀엽다

**Part of Speech:** 형용사
**Level:** TOPIK 2급

**Definition:** 귀엽다 — Cute; adorable.

## Examples

- "귀엽다" — Cute
- "너 귀엽다" — You're cute
- "귀여운 강아지" — Cute puppy

## Related Terms

- [[예쁘다]] — pretty
- [[멋있다]] — cool
- [[착하다]] — kind

## Cultural Notes

**한국에서 매우 흔하고 안전한 칭찬**. 연애 초기부터 사용 가능.

**사용 범위**:
- 여성 → 남성/여성 모두에게
- 남성 → 여성에게 (자주)
- 남성 → 남성에게 (드물지만 친한 사이에서 가능)

**MZ세대**: "귀엽다"의 사용 폭이 넓어짐 (옷, 캐릭터, 행동 모두).

착하다.md.body:
# 착하다

**Part of Speech:** 형용사
**Level:** TOPIK 2급

**Definition:** 착하다 — Kind; good-hearted.

## Examples

- "착하다" — Kind
- "너무 착해" — You're so kind

만나서-반가워요.md.title: 만나서-반가워요
만나서-반가워요.md.body:
# 만나서-반가워요

**Part of Speech:** 표현 (인사)
**Definition:** "만나서 반가워요" — Standard polite greeting on first meeting.

**형식:** 만나서 반가워요. (만나서 + 반갑다 + -(으)세요/-어요)

## Usage

처음 만났을 때 사용. 그 후 "잘 부탁드려요" 또는 "잘 부탁해"로.

## 변형

- **만나서 반가워요** — 표준 (격식)
- **만나서 반가워** — 반말 (친한 사이)
- **반가워요** — 단독 사용 (좀 더 친근)
- **만나서 영광이에요** — 격식

## Cultural Notes

**대화 흐름**:
1. 만나서 반가워요 (만나서 반갑습니다)
2. 제 이름은 ○○입니다 (저는 ○○)
3. 잘 부탁드려요 (잘 부탁해)

**응답**: 상대도 같은 패턴으로 답함.

**같은 사람에게 두 번 사용하지 않음**. 두 번째 만남부터는
"안녕하세요", "오랜만이에요".

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-kr]] — 자기소개
- TOPIK 1급
- 국립국어원

같이-밥-먹을래.md.title: 같이-밥-먹을래
같이-밥-먹을래.md.body:
# 같이-밥-먹을래

**Part of Speech:** 표현 (초대)
**Definition:** "같이 밥 먹을래?" — Most common casual date invitation.

**형식:** 같이 밥 먹을래? (같이 + 밥 + 먹다 + -을래?)

## Usage

**가장 흔한 첫 데이트 제안**. 부담 적음, 자연스러움.

## 변형

- **같이 밥 먹을래?** — 캐주얼 (친한 사이)
- **같이 밥 먹을래요?** — 정중
- **밥 같이 먹을까?** — 더 캐주얼
- **식사 같이 하실래요?** — 격식

## Cultural Notes

**"밥"의 의미**: 단순한 끼니 이상의 의미. "밥 먹자" = "시간 보내자"
= "알아 가자".

**응답**:
- 긍정: "좋아!" / "응, 좋아"
- 보류: "언제?" (구체화)
- 거절: "미안, 그날 안 돼"

**"밥"이 연애의 시작**: 많은 커플이 "같이 밥 먹자"에서 시작.
영화, 드라마, 현실 모두.

## Related Expressions

- [[너-자꾸-생각나]] — 썸 표현
- [[나-너-좋아해]] — 고백

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-kr]] — 초대
- 응답하라 1988

너-자꾸-생각나.md.title: 너-자꾸-생각나
너-자꾸-생각나.md.body:
# 너-자꾸-생각나

**Part of Speech:** 표현 (썸)
**Definition:** "너 자꾸 생각나" — Classic 썸 expression, "I keep thinking about you".

**형식:** 너 자꾸 생각나.

## Usage

**썸 단계의 표준 표현**. 호감을 은유적으로 표현.

## 변형

- **너 자꾸 생각나** — 기본
- **너 생각 많이 나** — 강조
- **자꾸 니가 떠올라** — 더 부드러움
- **자꾸 보고 싶어** — 더 진한 호감
- **보고 싶어** — 직진

## Cultural Notes

**한국 썸 문화의 필수 표현**. 드라마/영화에서 매우 흔함.

**단계**:
1. "자주 생각해" — 가벼운 호감
2. "자꾸 생각나" — 호감 (썸)
3. "보고 싶어" — 강한 호감
4. "너무 보고 싶어" — 연인

**언어별 비교**:
- 영어: "I keep thinking about you"
- 일본어: "ずっとあなたのこと考えてる"
- 스페인어: "No puedo dejar de pensar en ti"

## Related Expressions

- [[같이-밥-먹을래]] — 초대
- [[나-너-좋아해]] — 고백
- [[손-잡아도-돼]] — 신체 진전

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-kr]] — 썸
- 별에서 온 그대
- 사랑의 불시착

나-너-좋아해.md.title: 나-너-좋아해
나-너-좋아해.md.body:
# 나-너-좋아해

**Part of Speech:** 표현 (고백)
**Definition:** "나 너 좋아해" — Standard direct confession.

**형식:** 나 너 좋아해.

## Usage

**고백의 정석**. 명확하고 직접적.

## 변형

- **나 너 좋아해** — 직접 (가장 흔함)
- **나 너 좋아해, 사귀자** — 사귀자 추가
- **너 좋아해** — 부드러움
- **나는 너를 좋아해** — 좀 더 격식
- **내 사람 돼줄래?** — 부드러운 고백

## Cultural Notes

**고백 시점**: 보통 썸 1-3개월 후.

**위치 선택**: 공공장소 (카페, 공원), 너무 공개적이지 않게.
밤이 더 로맨틱.

**응답**:
- 긍정: "나도 좋아해" — I like you too
- 보류: "생각 좀 해볼게" — Let me think
- 거절: "미안, 우리 친구로 지내자" — Sorry, let's stay friends

**"사귀자" 추가의 효과**: 고백 + 사귀기 제안을 한 번에. 직접적이지만
효율적.

## Related Expressions

- [[같이-밥-먹을래]] — 초대
- [[너-자꾸-생각나]] — 썸
- [[손-잡아도-돼]] — 신체 진전

## Sources

- [[dating-romance-kr]] — 고백
- 응답하라 1988
- 별에서 온 그대

Los primeros niños que vieron el promontorio oscuro y sigiloso que se acercaba por el mar, se hicieron la ilusión de que era un barco enemigo. Después vieron que no llevaba banderas ni arboladura, y pensaron que fuera una ballena. Pero cuando quedó varado en la playa le quitaron los matorrales de sargazos, los filamentos de medusas y los restos de cardúmenes y naufragios que llevaba encima, y solo entonces descubrieron que era un ahogado.

Habían jugado con él toda la tarde, enterrándolo y desenterrándolo en la arena, cuando alguien los vio por casualidad y dio la voz de alarma en el pueblo.

Curated reference for 恋愛(연애) conversation in Korean.
**15세 이상 권장** (15+ 권장) 콘텐츠: 썸(설렘), 고백, 연인 사이의
친밀한 대화. Explicit한 표현은 제외.

한국 연애 문화의 특징:
- **썸 (ssum, "some")**: 교제 전 설렘 단계. "우리 지금 뭐야?"가 흔한 질문.
- **고백 (告白)**: 公式(공식) 행사로 문화적 중요성.
- **존댓말 → 반말 전환**: 연인 사이의 중요한 시점.
- **커뮤케이션 스타일**: 직접적 표현, 애칭(호칭) 사용, 질투/관심 표현.
